```markdown
# Andrew — Fractional CFO Discovery Call

**Source file:** Andrew Probelm.pdf
**Participants:** Tabarak (Vantum) · Andrew (Fractional CFO)

---

**Tabarak:** Recording in progress. Hey, Andrew, how's it going? Can you hear me?

**Andrew:** Hey, good morning. Good evening from me.

**Tabarak:** I'm actually based out of Bangkok, Thailand, so I'm a little in the future.

**Andrew:** Okay, that's halfway around the world.

**Tabarak:** Yeah, what time is it over there right now?

**Andrew:** I'm sorry?

**Tabarak:** Oh, I said what time is it over there right now?

**Andrew:** So, I'm in Colorado. It's 8:30 a.m.

**Tabarak:** Okay, so it's 9:30 p.m. over here. Pretty much halfway around the world. Started your day and I'm about to wrap it up here pretty soon.

**Andrew:** Yeah, I hope so.

**Tabarak:** Hey, well, I really appreciate you taking the time to hop on a call with me. That's awesome. I really do appreciate that. I guess I can give you a little bit of background about me and kind of why we're on this call today. I got the time to connect and get some time on your calendar, but my name is Tabarak. My last adult job, I was a regional vice president at DocuSign, was it back 2022? So, I've been doing sales for about 20 years or so and was working at Verizon, Apple, and then DocuSign. And in 2022, they let me go. I was in the first round of layoffs. But fortunately, it was actually a very good situation for me. I'd been wanting to get out. I didn't get the opportunity. I had golden handcuffs. And so, that essentially got me to travel the world. So, I went to Europe. I went to a little bit of Africa and then Southeast Asia. And then, I obviously haven't left.

**Andrew:** Okay, okay. Yeah, it was a cool place. Have you been?

**Tabarak:** I did as a kid.

**Andrew:** So, my dad was in international trade. And he took our family once when I was a little kid around the world. And Bangkok was one of the places we went.

**Tabarak:** Wow, how old were you?

**Andrew:** I was nine years old.

**Tabarak:** Oh man, you remembered it too.

**Andrew:** So, yeah, the Thai people are very, very nice. I like Thailand a lot.

**Tabarak:** Oh yeah, super nice. Amazing weather. I mean, I haven't seen a winter. Honestly, I went back once in all this time in March. But that's like the first winter I've been back in years. And to be honest, I don't miss it. I'm from Seattle.

**Andrew:** Oh, okay. My brother was from Seattle.

**Tabarak:** Yeah, I mean, it's not the coldest. But when you're coming from a tropical place like this, 50 degrees is just ridiculous. Well, so I ended up coming here. And after a couple years of traveling, I just realized, you know what? I actually really love to work. I can't just do the vacation thing forever. I reached out to one of my buddies. He worked at DocuSign as well. He was a director over there. And we both had started companies in our past lives. But we both realized the one thing that we weren't good at was marketing. So, we spent about a year async from Seattle to Bangkok learning how to do copywriting and YouTube and LinkedIn and all of that. And as we worked closer together, we realized, you know what? Let's take this chance. He quit his 300K a year job and moved to Bangkok with his wife. So, we started to do this thing where we're going to start a business. And obviously, while we were trying to firm up the exact shape of the business through the idea maze, we realized, okay, let's take on some clients. And so, we did some consulting work, which led to doing more in-depth fractional work. And we did some fractional CRO and go-to-market work. And at that time, we were managing multiple clients and we're trying to run this business or start this business. And we had this juxtaposition of, wow, like how do I manage my clients and also do the best that I can on my end, right, in starting my business? And that was the first idea of like, hey, maybe we do have a business here, right? So, that led me to reaching out to you. You're the actual 52nd fractional I've spoken to. And we really just wanted to find out, you know, what's this problem space? Are the same problems happening with every fractional? And so, for this call, I wanted to kind of go over, you know, just learn a little bit about you, learn about, you know, your time as a fractional, any challenges you might be seeing. And then I can share all the problems I'm seeing from other fractionals, how they're solving them. And then our approach from our solution, from our software, and kind of get some feedback from you, think, hey, is this something that sounds like might solve that problem set if you have that, right? And if that sounds good and you're interested, we're happy to set up some time to do a demonstration of the product. And from there, if you like the product, we're inviting a select group of about 10 or 12 fractionals in our customer development program, where we give them the product, and then we finish developing it around their core specs. So we fit it directly towards them. How's that sound?

**Andrew:** Cool.

**Tabarak:** Why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself, Andrew?

**Andrew:** Yeah, that's good. Yeah. So what would you like to know?

**Tabarak:** I'd like to, oh, what got you into fractional work? What kind of fractional work are you doing now? How's your business? How's everything going?

**Andrew:** Sure. So I decided to go into fractional work mainly because I wanted more independence. I had a couple of corporate jobs that had fairly toxic work dynamics and was just tired of being a W-2 CFO in that kind of situation. So really having that independence was the big driver. So yeah, I'm a fractional CFO, so I'm doing chief financial officer type work. At this point, I'm just solo. I don't have any team members. Ultimately, I might decide to add additional team members, but for now, I'm pretty happy just being solo. So I don't know that I have any particular pain points.

**Tabarak:** How many clients do you have now?

**Andrew:** Three.

**Tabarak:** Is that your ideal amount that you like?

**Andrew:** I think I could handle more, so maybe one more.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. What does that cost you in hours of work during the week? Do you have a set amount you like? Are you a workaholic? Do you carve out a specific amount of time and you're happy with that?

**Andrew:** Yeah. So at this point in my career, I'm not looking to push it really hard. I'm happy maybe working 35, no more than 40 hours a week. I'm not looking to burn the midnight oil and work 50 plus hours a week.

**Tabarak:** You've been there, done that, right?

**Andrew:** Yeah, that's not my thing. So yeah, I think maybe one more client would be about as much as I want to handle. So I guess the one area that I haven't totally solved to my satisfaction, it's... I wouldn't describe it as a problem per se, but as a fractional, you're having to shift gears between actual client work and business development work. And shifting gears back and forth between those two zones is a little bit tricky to manage. I find it hard to just constantly be going back and forth between those two areas. So I think trying to structure my day where there's uninterrupted client work time and then there's uninterrupted business development time in different chunks, that's what works best for me rather than constantly flipping back and forth.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. The context switching can really damage you in regards to productivity overall, right?

**Andrew:** Yeah.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. How do you plan your week? Do you go into it on like a Sunday and you plan out your entire week? Do you use a calendar or are you like a scratch note person?

**Andrew:** Yeah, I use... I have a calendar. I just try to block out time for different activities. So I have a pretty good sense of overall when I'm going to focus on different things. I use a CRM called HubSpot.

**Tabarak:** Cool.

**Andrew:** Yeah. And yeah, I guess in the business development zone, one thing I am kind of looking at is finding someone to help me write high quality content for marketing.

**Tabarak:** Yeah.

**Andrew:** I used a service for a while that was kind of an AI written product that I just didn't... I wasn't happy with. It was very generic. It sounded like all the other posts on LinkedIn. So yeah, I've actually been exploring finding someone who won't use AI that is really writing higher quality content to help me do some more posting on LinkedIn.

**Tabarak:** For sure. How do you find your clients now? Is it mainly through LinkedIn or is it referral based?

**Andrew:** Referral so far. So far referral. I've done a lot of LinkedIn, reaching out to people, trying to engage people in conversations. So far that hasn't worked super well.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. Question for you. I guess I didn't ask you, how long have you been doing fractional work now?

**Andrew:** About a year.

**Tabarak:** About a year. Okay. And so this is typically when I hear a lot of the referrals, you come in, you have a pretty good amount of referrals. And then at some point they might dry up a little bit, but you're just trying to find that balance. Do you have a set of, I guess it's only been a year, so you may not have enough data points, but how long is your client length in retention typically?

**Andrew:** Yeah. Like you said, I haven't been doing it long enough to say.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. So right now the ones that you have is pretty much what you started off with.

**Andrew:** Yeah.

**Tabarak:** Okay. That makes sense. And when I hear the question about managing business development and work time, it usually comes in a couple of buckets. One is specifically into go prospect and find new clients. And then the other one is my schedule is pretty full in capacity and I just don't even have the time. Or my system isn't available for me to do that. Is that the second bucket? Is that not you? It sounds like you can—

**Andrew:** Yeah. No, I don't say it's a time issue yet. Yeah. It's just finding meaningful, productive things. I've done a lot of face-to-face networking activities to join various networking groups and I'm continuing to do that. But so far, those haven't paid off.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. And here's the other question. AI helps with speed. Do you have the time to carve out to learn how to write or write consistently? And just have that as one of the things that you do is you just kind of do it on your own?

**Andrew:** Yeah. That's kind of where I'm kind of landing is that it's going to be incumbent on me to do some of that writing. Yeah. I'm just not happy with what I've had other people doing for me.

**Tabarak:** It's totally true. I mean, I struggle with it myself, right? And it's because I'm starting a business, I'm doing all these other things, I'm doing these interviews, we're working on a product and it has to become a priority because there has to be a why behind it. And if you don't have a why behind it, it just falls off at some point, right? And if that why, usually it happens when you don't have any clients and it's like, I have to do this to get clients. And then it becomes urgent. I see you're trying to get ahead of it before that happens, right?

**Andrew:** Sure. Sure.

**Tabarak:** That's cool. That's cool. I don't think you're wrong for trying to find a solution to speed up the process. AI writing is just not there yet. Here's one thing that can help. If you write enough good pieces, AI will speed up the process, right? Because then it can get closer and closer to your... You can say, hey, here's what I wrote. Let's write like this, right?

**Andrew:** Sure. Sure.

**Tabarak:** Rate it out of 10 and then you do that a couple of iterations. And the more often you do that, it'll learn a formula and then you can set a formula for how you write.

**Andrew:** Yeah.

**Tabarak:** That's kind of a way that I would operate in that regard. But the reality is you do need to just segment off hard-earned time, right? To get that writing on there. And to be honest, I'm in the depths of AI right now. So every three months, things get changed, like literally three months, right? So I would honestly think in the next six months, there will be something pretty good out. At least get you part of that.

**Andrew:** What AI tools do you favor?

**Tabarak:** Wow. So I use about a dozen. We have a new rule in our company. We do not do annual subscriptions. We only do monthly because everything changes so fast on a dime. We use ChatGPT and Claude. We moved away from ChatGPT, right? So Claude is our primary. I had to move all my memories out of... Claude has this really cool thing. It can take your memories. You go to the settings, say, I want my memories from another LLM. It'll send you to ChatGPT and then you can take all your memories out. But I do keep two because I make them fight each other, right? So that's the way to use AI. You use one, you put it in the prompt into one, then you take it out, grade it in the other one, take it out, grade it back in the other one, and you get a higher quality of output. So those are two. I don't know if you do any videos. There's something called vidIQ. If you do one minute videos, they do really good with YouTube. They kind of have the YouTube formula. It's called vidIQ, V-I-D.

**Andrew:** VidIQ, vidIQ.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. So it's another one where they're not perfect. The one thing is whoever made it, and I actually spent a year doing marketing. I actually took a $7,000 YouTube course. And after learning how to do that the manual way without ChatGPT, and then we got vidIQ, I realized, oh, whoever made this knows the formula for YouTube, right? So how you write your hooks, how you lead people in, the packaging, the thumbnail. So they embed a little bit of that in there so you can write your script. It can pull from your LinkedIn, take your experiences, and then make it into a one-minute story or whatever it is. And then you could use that to record, right?

**Andrew:** Sure. Sure.

**Tabarak:** What other tools do you use right now for your business?

**Andrew:** No, just in general. I mean, obviously, you have to use Excel, right? The spreadsheet, maybe Google.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. Do you use anything to manage your week, or are you just doing it more documentation on a piece of paper?

**Andrew:** I think I use a lot of documentation. Sorry. Do I use anything to manage what?

**Tabarak:** Oh, manage your week, like outside of your calendar.

**Andrew:** Oh, outside of my calendar, no. I mean, like I said, that's CRM.

**Tabarak:** Okay. And you use it just for the CRM, or is there other modules you use for...

**Andrew:** No. At the moment, I'm really just using the CRM.

**Tabarak:** That makes sense. It's the sales version of the website.

**Andrew:** Okay. It's not really fancy.

**Tabarak:** Oh, yeah. They got so many of them. Yeah. Okay. Here's a question I'd ask you. If you had a magic wand right now, and you could wave it and solve one core problem in your company that would just unblock something, what would that be?

**Andrew:** I think the biggest thing I'm focused on is really just having a good, consistent pipeline and opportunities. Right now, it's pretty bumpy. I can go long periods without any new client inquiries, and then I'll get a couple at a time. So, I'd like a more steady state of new client opportunities. And my gut says that that's going to come by posting good quality content on LinkedIn. It seems like that is the best way for me to market myself. I've looked into email campaigns, and it seems like the success rate with the email campaigns is very low. You have to have a huge volume to just get one quality lead. So, I think it's that pipeline flow is probably the area that I need to address most.

**Tabarak:** Okay. That makes sense. I'll give you a quick rundown, and then I'll go through these challenges. So, what I've been seeing in the client, essentially the feast or famine problem, right? You have it. You don't. It's lumpy. It's called the feast or famine cycle. I've seen a few problems. One problem is that we find fractionals that either are really busy, and they can't, like I said, they can't find the time. The other one is they're not really salespeople, right? So, if you're not on the sales side, it's like a black box. It's like, how do I get these clients, right? Part of it is there's things that happen before you reach out to people, and there's things that happen after you reach out to people. A lot of people focus on the after part. Let me go reach out. So, a question for you is, what's your promise? What do you deliver? What's your one-sentence pitch to your clientele?

**Andrew:** So, I would say there are two aspects to that. There's kind of my industry focus, and then there's my work service focus. So, my core value, I think, as a fractional CFO from an industry standpoint is that I have deep experience in manufacturing and healthcare. So, those two industries are what I really need to lean into. I have 12 plus years of experience both in healthcare and in manufacturing, which I think differentiates me as a fractional CFO. I would say the typical fractional CFO is chasing software as a service, more cutting-edge, newer, more tech-focused businesses, whereas I'm a little bit more old school. It's healthcare and manufacturing is where I have experience. But then on the service delivery side, I think my value is that I have very deep analytical skills. I'm not a CPA. I have an MBA in finance, so I don't approach CFO work the way an accountant would. I'm much more analytical and strategic in my focus. So, I'm trying to position myself as someone who can really help look at situations, analyze them, and provide a path for growth or process improvement, as opposed to just reporting on the financial numbers and maybe giving just generic advice on how to improve things.

**Tabarak:** Makes sense.

**Andrew:** If that makes sense.

**Tabarak:** No, that makes sense. And I can see the challenge now. I see where the issue is very clearly. Here's a question. I've worked in healthcare. I've not worked in manufacturing. I would guess that the customer in healthcare is not the same customer in manufacturing, correct?

**Andrew:** Oh, yeah, for sure. Yeah, those are two distinct things.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. And so, they would probably need two very distinct messages, correct?

**Andrew:** Yes.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. So, what I've seen is this actual situation exactly is a lot of fractionals are coming in essentially with one approach, but for two different segments, right? And that one approach is correct. Now, this is the crappy part about it. It's just marketing now, right? How do you say your approach, but in two different flavors for two people that are experiencing pain at the exact moment that they need you, right? And so, the question is, when is that person in healthcare having so much pain that when you send them a message, they're like, yeah, I want to talk to you. And when is that person that's in manufacturing in so much pain? There's one point. There's probably five problems that each of them have, but one of them is the number one. For you, it's your lumpy client flow. So, when you get a message that might suggest that you can have some help in that regard, you might want to talk to your person, right? So, I would try to find what are the biggest five things maybe from the clients that you have that you're solving for your healthcare people and then for your manufacturing people and then drill down to that core pain moment and speak to just that very crisp one sentence, right? Hey, I can help you in healthcare. For me, when I was in healthcare for life sciences, their issue was going through the CFR Part 11 was a big issue, right? So, being able to manage software that utilizes CFR Part 11 so that they can go through their iteration phases much faster, which allows them to get to their drugs faster, right? And what is that pain in either of those areas? And when you go and lock that, this is the hard part, much harder than sending the message out, right? Then dialing that down into a sentence and then also dialing that down into what you talk about, right? So, now you need content pillars. Now you have your five problems in retail, you have your problems in healthcare and you only talk around those problems in your posts, right? And when you send out a message to connect, you're not connecting to close a deal. You're just connecting to find out if they have that problem, right?

**Andrew:** Right, right. So, I have kind of done that already in the healthcare side. Like if you looked at the blog page of my website, you'll see posts that are specifically tailored for healthcare problems.

**Tabarak:** Okay.

**Andrew:** I haven't done it for manufacturing yet, but that is kind of my roadmap to develop some content more manufacturing focused. But yeah, I essentially have done that on the healthcare side. I guess I need more volume and time.

**Tabarak:** For sure, for sure. It's the volume, it's the time. And then also I've been talking to a lot of fractionals and I think a big thing too is when you get that dialed down to a one DM or one message, then you can reach out to a lot of it. If you know what that is, you know what the pain is, then you'll probably know who the specific ICP is. But for healthcare, who do you go after?

**Andrew:** So, I'm targeting specifically physician groups and ambulatory surgery centers. I'm not talking about like hospital systems, you know, really big health services. Privately held, independent physician groups and surgery centers.

**Tabarak:** Okay, okay. That's your bread and butter.

**Andrew:** I mean, that's where my experience lies. Yeah. Because I was a CFO for a few physician owned independent healthcare groups.

**Tabarak:** Okay, that's awesome. Yeah, I think you might have it. I mean, the thing is that the reality is it's going to be volume and then just condensing your message down, you know?

**Andrew:** Yeah.

**Tabarak:** Until I eventually build the product that allows us to do that. But I'll go over some of the key problems. Can you see my screen right now?

**Andrew:** Yeah.

**Tabarak:** Okay, so these are the number one problems I've seen for fractionals. Number one, feast or famine cycle. You said it, so I won't even say more on that. Secondary one, losing client trust because they have no system for protecting commitments when fire drills hit midweek. Number three, feeling overwhelmed, anxious, or guilty because you're constantly worried about letting yourself or clients down. Number four, urgent work blowing up in the middle of the week, so you keep busy, but you don't move forward on your bigger business goals. And then lastly, stitching together a lot of different paid systems, doing a lot of admin, and it doesn't sound like that's kind of your core problem, but I've seen that.

**Andrew:** No.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. Do any of these strike a chord outside of the stuck in feast or famine?

**Andrew:** Yeah, that's the one that's probably most top of mind for me. Like I said, I haven't lost a client yet, but I know it's going to happen eventually.

**Tabarak:** Yeah.

**Andrew:** And then that's why I'm concerned about having a certain pipeline. So I've got back to zero. Let's see. Losing client trust because you have no... I mean, I haven't really experienced the second one yet. Feeling overwhelmed, anxious, guilty. I mean, I wouldn't say that's a big issue for me. It's just a natural state. Let's see. The fourth one can be urgent work blows up, so you kind of are focused on putting out the fire and you sometimes are putting off bigger business goals. Yeah, that fourth one, it's I can see being relevant too.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. And then stitching together multiple key tools.

**Andrew:** Yeah, I don't have too many. Like I said, I've got HubSpot. I am looking at a tool called Fathom. I don't know if you know Fathom.

**Tabarak:** I've heard of that.

**Andrew:** Yeah. Reporting. It integrates with financial accounting platforms to help automate some report generation and kind of financial planning and analysis type tools. I'm looking at that.

**Tabarak:** Cool. Here's a... I'm sorry, did we end at 10 p.m. or 10 or at the top of the hour?

**Andrew:** I mean, yeah, we ended now, but I don't have something immediately.

**Tabarak:** Okay. We just got a couple more minutes. Okay. Yeah. So these are the solutions that I've been hearing. They're truly not full solutions, but telling yourself you'll do outbound time on your calendar and then giving it away at the moment a client needs pops up. Spending, waking up, looking at your list, spending 30, 60 minutes triaging what the priority for the day is, figuring out what's on fire, compensating by working nights and weekends reactively, not strategically. It's okay if you want to work nights and weekends, but not if you're forced to, right? Quarterly goals get scheduled and then pushed week after week. You haven't known this, but a lot of people have made the ring around the rosies of every single software coming back and it doesn't solve their problems. And then lastly, using AI to try to solve problems, but they're not really developers and the context memory gets full and then they lose their context and things just don't really, it just really rickety is what I've been getting out of that. Do any of these align with your experience at all?

**Andrew:** Yeah, the first one happens where, you know, marketing business development oriented and then I end up focusing on a client need instead. That happens. I wouldn't say it's all the time, but it does happen. Second one, not really. Third one, not really. I'm not so busy yet that I'm working nights and weekends. Let's see, quarterly goals get stretched. Yeah, I mean, sort of. I'm in my first year, so my goals are still fairly, you know, in the early phase.

**Tabarak:** Yeah, you're easing into them. You're trying to figure out your baseline.

**Andrew:** Yeah. That spreadsheet required constant manual coordination. Yeah, I mean, that's a little bit what I'm looking to solve with this Fathom reporting tool is it can help automate some connections between the accounting software and reports.

**Tabarak:** Yeah, for sure.

**Andrew:** And then AI. Yeah, I'm just not, I haven't utilized AI very thoroughly yet.

**Tabarak:** Makes sense.

**Andrew:** That's a future goal.

**Tabarak:** Yeah, for sure. So here's our approach, right? So our system's kind of like you can imagine is like Google Maps for your workweek. Essentially what it is, it's a fractional command center. So you don't have many systems, but your calendars, your emails, any other system, we essentially pull all your tasks into one centralized location. And what we do is we keep track of your capacity. So we understand how much time you actually have. And what we do is we measure that by something called stakeholders, which are anything that eats up your time. So client A, client B, doing your marketing, going for trying to find new clients. And then we also pair that with your goals. And so essentially what we do is we use an algorithm that actually creates a score for all of your tasks. So we understand when you plan at the beginning of the week, it's not the same plan on a Wednesday, new things pop up, right? So whenever new things pop up, it actually scores it and then it reshuffles it so that it's always giving you the most important thing, not just by urgency or deadline. Of course, that's very marked high, but also by your goals. Are you doing what you said was important for your business, right? And so one question I always ask fractionals, I'm really excited to ask you is how do you, I usually get two answers, but how do you know when you've had a successful week?

**Andrew:** I would define a successful week as one where I'm able to devote time to both client work and business development work without having to shuffle things around and put out fires. And it's more or less the week goes as planned and I've moved project work forward. It's not just addressing emergencies, it's actually making progress towards whatever.

**Tabarak:** Yeah. I hear two answers and it's kind of like it's usually not really a quantifiable thing, but more of a gut feeling or I've scratched a couple of things off my list that were important. I hear that part. The other answer, which was very surprising to me, is a lot of people said, I haven't thought about that. This is the first time they've ever even thought about that process, right? So what we do at the beginning of your week, we get all your tasks, we essentially just show how many hours you're in for, what's important, what breaks down. So I have 30% to this client, 20% to this client. My effective rate is this. These are the goals that I want and I'm putting my time towards this. And by the end of the week, you get to see how that shakes out. You get a report, it shows you on a dashboard, everything shows you what you've completed by task, what percentage and are you on, did you do what you said you were going to do, right? And it also gives you some feedback on how to improve for the following week, right? And upon completion, it also even marks off first principally, like, what are some additional tasks you're going to do based on the stuff you've completed? And it already throws it into your backlog. What do you think about this approach, whether it's for you overall? You don't hit all the markers for the challenges, but do you have any feedback on this approach, any thoughts, any questions?

**Andrew:** Yeah, I would say for me, those don't sound like things that I personally need a lot of help with. Like those kind of fall in the zone of organizational planning type pain points, which I don't think is my real issue. I can see that other fractionals may struggle with that. I think I'm a pretty well-organized person, so those aren't really pain points for me.

**Tabarak:** I don't think so, because typically this approach really does fit when you mark five out of five on this side, on the challenges, right? And we're building towards that, right? The one thing I'll let you know is this is our foundation layer. You can imagine if we can get all your tasks, we understand all your goals, all your stakeholders, and we're always able to give you the right thing to do next, our next thing on top of that is building playbooks that we can embed into your system. One playbook would be how to find new clients, right? And that would be a module that helps you understand your promise, understand what ICP, what messages, how many messages you send out, and then it would go on your calendar, and then you would actually have that embedded on your... You don't have to think about it. The system knows, hey, you have a client that might be coming to term. You need to do 12 calls this week, right? And then it would implant that and put the messaging and stuff. But I think we have to get this part right first before we can add in the other modules on top of that.

**Andrew:** Yeah. So are you targeting solo fractional consultants or are you targeting also firms that have teams of fractionals?

**Tabarak:** Our core hypothesis is if we can get it right for a solo, the teams is just going to be icing on the cake, right? So we do want to move into teams after this. And what that means is now you'll know everybody's capacity. You'll know everybody's bottlenecks. You'll be able to see on both ends, transfer skills and knowledge. And then essentially, I would love this for my team when we build it internally is I can see all the bottlenecks throughout my entire organization from the individual up to my report, right? So yeah, right now it's a solo approach. And then we will be moving to having the AI do work for you. So it learns your tasks as you're doing them. So we break down everything into steps. Eventually, the AI will then be able to say, hey, I can do this. And you can push some of that capacity over to the AI and do that. And then we'll move into teams right after that.

**Andrew:** Gotcha. Okay. Any thoughts about that? No. Yeah. For the foreseeable future, I'm just going to stay solo. I don't plan to build out a huge team at this point. I mean, my sense is the work lifestyle equation really shifts at that point.

**Tabarak:** It does.

**Andrew:** Me as the primary owner would be less focused on client work and more focused on just like operations and business development work. And I don't know that that's really where I want to be spending my time.

**Tabarak:** Could you imagine a world where eventually, and this is the thing, in the future, we would hope that solo operators now get the help of their AI. So now they get to do the tasks that they like to do. And the AI can offshoot all the other tasks. For you, you're one of our, I can see a division of our customers at one point, and I put them in different buckets. The bucket we usually deal with are people that have four, five, six clients, want to work 60 hours a week, want to do a business on the side. But then we have another bucket. I just transitioned. I got out of corporate work. I want to hang out with my daughter, my family. I want to chill. And I want to work because I love to solve problems. And for them, I've learned that they would take on more clients if they could do equal or less work and get paid more because our AI helps them out. They'd be like, yeah, I'll raise my hand for that. But I don't want to work more hours, but if you can give me more clients and I get paid more and I'm not doing much more work, I'd do that.

**Andrew:** That describes me more, the second one.

**Tabarak:** It'll be some time till we get to you. But when we do, I'd be happy to reach back out to you at that point. I want to tackle the hard stuff first so that you can reap all the rewards when we figure out anything from the hard people.

**Andrew:** Yeah. Yeah, no, that's cool. Yeah. I'd be happy to stay in touch with you.

**Tabarak:** For sure, man. I appreciate that. And if you have any thoughts or ideas about where you just want to throw a marketing idea over, I'm happy to take a look at it. This is the thing. I love being a founder, but also I was a fractional. I have this helping nature. I do want to help. If you ever send me a message, you say, hey, man, I have this promise. I think this might work. I'll give you any feedback as well.

**Andrew:** Awesome. Okay. Well, thank you.

**Tabarak:** Of course, man. Andrew, you have a good rest of your day. I really appreciate the time.

**Andrew:** All right. Have a good day, man. Sounds good to me. Okay. Nice to meet you.

**Tabarak:** Nice to meet you too. Bye-bye.

**Andrew:** Okay. Bye.
```

ELENA: Good to meet you, too.

TABARAK: Is Dubai or Spain home?

ELENA: Dubai, but home is like permanent in Spain. And then Dubai is where I've been for the past few years. So I guess it's two homes.

ELENA: Oh, yeah, of course.

TABARAK: Yeah, so your timing's amazing.

ELENA: It's actually 5.30 PM. Great. Well, in Dubai, oh, no, Dubai would have been even closer. So, yeah, we're okay either way.

TABARAK: I love that. Well, I appreciate you taking the time, you know, answering my message and setting up the time. I wanted to give you a heads-up of kind of what we're doing here. I'll give you a little background about me. I'm Tabarik. I am the CEO and co-founder of a company that we're starting right now called Vantum. Recently, I guess most recently in my corporate world, I was a regional vice president at a company called DocuSign.

ELENA: Oh, yeah, I know DocuSign. Yeah, I use them.

TABARAK: I've been doing sales for about 20 years. I ended there. I took a trip around. It was the first round of layoffs. I got promoted a few times, and then I got promoted into a team that didn't need a leader, you know? Fortunately, I had the golden handcuffs. I wasn't going to leave. I wanted to leave. It was perfect timing. I got my severance. I got everything the best way possible, right? So I went and traveled the world, and I did that for a couple years, and I just realized I love to work. I can't. I can't be on vacation on my butt every day. It was not good for me. So I called one of my friends from DocuSign. He was a director over there. We both had started businesses on the side before, and we started working together. We learned, oh, wow, we need to learn marketing. Regret it, sales, but marketing is a whole other thing. If we want to start a business, that's the big hole we have. So we spent a year working together from across the world, learning YouTube and LinkedIn and copywriting and a lot of random stuff, and then I convinced him to move to Bangkok, quit his job. $400,000 a year, quit his job, come here and start a business, right? So in that time, we realized, okay, well, we want to start this business. We're really important on purpose and having a line-of-sight vision that we both agree upon. So we said, hey, let's work on some different projects. What we can do is we have specialties. So let's do some consulting while we figure out our business, right? Next thing we know, we take on some clients. We're fractional consultants. Then we realize we're trying to start a business, and we're trying to manage clients, and we're trying to manage our time, and we're putting off the stuff that's important to take care of fires, and it's like, all right, well, there's a problem here. So is it just us?

ELENA: I've been there. I know exactly what you're talking about. I've been there. You could be narrating my life right now.

TABARAK: So we assume, okay, well, if this is happening to us, we know this. There has to be other people. So we just get on the phone. We start calling probably maybe three or four dozen deep in conversations with fractionals, right, from CFOs, CMOs, CHROs, and just narrowing it down and getting people's understanding. Essentially, what we did is we collected a lot of challenges. We hear a lot of people. We figured out how they're solving stuff. So today we kind of want to do the same thing, kind of learn about your adventure as a fractional, how you got here, what issues you might be having. I'll share some of the challenges, and at the end of the call, I'll also share what we're working on or kind of our approach. And the idea of this is if this all sounds good, you have a lot of the problems that I'm narrating of your life story. If it sounds like it's something that might be interesting to solve, we're happy to invite you to do it. We'll present the demonstration of the product. And then a few people who enjoy the product have the problems. We're inviting them to join our customer development program. So we give the product to them for free over a course of, I think, 10 weeks or so, and then we build the product around maybe 10 or 12 very core customers and say, hey, let's build it for you guys. You're a perfect customer, right? And so that's the whole purpose of these interviews that we're doing.

ELENA: Okay, very interesting. Right.

TABARAK: So tell me a little bit about yourself, Elena. What brought you to Dubai and into your fractional work?

ELENA: Yeah, so I've had pretty much a very similar life to you where I was in corporate big jobs, possibly for 20 plus years, primarily in hospitality, in lifestyle and luxury hospitality brands. So I've held anything from SVP roles, marketing roles, global brand roles for some of the hospitality's biggest names. I've done regional roles, global roles, the whole thing. And then I kind of jumped ship about three years ago. I got called by an education group and they were like in Dubai. And they were like, look, we're looking for a senior vice president of marketing. You know, we don't want someone in education. We want someone in hospitality because of consumer centricity, blah, blah, blah. We want someone with brands experience. So it fit really well. I kind of knew that they were going through some IPO sort of work and I knew it was a bit risky, but I thought, OK, let me go. And I did it for about a year and a half and then IPO fell through. And very similar to you, they had to go to the banks and banks came in and changed all the leadership except for the son of the founder who remains. And we all left, you know, very well, you know, as you say, paid and whatever. I also about six months off to just spend time being a mom again, trying to get my family once again. And then I thought, like you, I need to do something. I can't just, you know, but I needed to be the right role. And a lot of the jobs were coming in from the U.S. and from Paris. And, you know, all the head offices of hospitality are in certain cities. I had the kids at an age where it was complicated to move. So I thought, you know what? I haven't found the right one. I'm going to start trying this fractional direction so that at least I'm doing something, getting some income in and waiting for that role to come. But it kicked off really well. So I started with a couple of projects in Saudi. They were primarily, which I'm still working on. They're primarily launching brands, hospitality brands, ground up, beautiful brand in a UNESCO protected site. And they have plans to expand globally. Then I started going in-house with a company called the Sun Secret Collection. They were an SME. Most of the clients I found that needed my sort of fractional work were SMEs, you know, starting up smaller companies where exactly like you, you know, they kind of like they grew to a certain point where at the beginning, everybody was doing everything. And then they come to a growth stage where that doesn't work anymore. And they need the right expertise to come in and do a bit of restructuring, put the processes in place, all the documentation that they need in place. And so that's really what I was doing for the Sun Secret Collection very, very well until about two weeks ago when the war started. And then the Italian Alfredo is a really great guy. He was like, listen, I need to put you on hold and I need to get rid of some of my staff, unfortunately. So that kind of is coming to an end. I'm still holding on to Saudi quite well. Saudi seems to be holding up. Dubai is in a different place. I think there's two ways it can go. One, it can go really well on the fractional side, because now they are going to start feeling the pain. Tourism has been hit brutally, unfortunately, and unfairly, to be honest, because I didn't feel safe in Dubai. It was just, you know, we're just kind of waiting to see where it goes. Schools are online. It's very complicated. But I see two opportunities in Dubai. One is that, you know, people will start laying off and will need expertise, maybe in a bit more agile way, more in and out when they need them kind of thing. So I'm waiting to see where that goes. And in the meantime, sort of carrying on with Saudi and building this brand, which is supposed to launch in October. We'll see if that happens or not. And that's where really I'm at, more or less.

TABARAK: Yeah. I love the versatility. Well, this is the thing. I mean, everyone I've talked to, you've built up enough skills in your life, right? And then you have an entrepreneurial nature as well, regardless. It's, hey, I'll go out and go figure this out, right? And it's a kind of scary thing to start off, right? I really love how you just, you found work pretty quickly. Did you go through your network or how did that work?

ELENA: Yeah, it was an old network. I mean, I literally have, I haven't even done a website yet, which probably I need to do because it's been in the making. Like if you go to the website, it says coming soon. And it's been coming for a year, but I didn't have time to pick up any other clients. So I was like, well, let me just focus on the work. It was very busy with these guys. So I was like, I'll worry about marketing myself later on. And I haven't really done that yet. I will start soon. But yeah, it was all through the network, all through the network. I literally didn't have to do much.

TABARAK: Yeah. So, okay. So question for you right now is aside from the Dubai situation, which no one had any control over that, right?

ELENA: Yeah.

TABARAK: How many clients did you have? What do you find is an ideal amount of clients for you?

ELENA: Look, I've had at the most about five in one go. And because it was just me, you don't have any more time. So I couldn't physically take on anybody else. It was like, okay, I'm closed. I can't. But I was at one point thinking, how do I scale this? How do I make it bigger? And I think there's something very interesting there in the sense that maybe there's a couple of different sort of tiering models of the business. Like one is my time. A bit like consultants do it. If you want your senior lawyer or your senior consultant, you pay X. And then if you have other needs, like you need me to step in and out, but you need, I don't know, other team people to come in, like you need a graphic designer or an SEO specialist, I now have that freelance network where I can pull them in and push them into your company. But I haven't really, again, you know, very much in that direction yet. But max was five. The least I've had was about three. Now I have three.

TABARAK: Yeah. And are they all retainers or are you doing retainer and project or how does that work?

ELENA: So with the Sun Secret Collection, it was almost like I was in-house. So I was part of their team. It was just two days a week. But it was a very different sort of payment model. I had, you know, a salary every month and I was accountable for the team and, you know, they'd call me on Saturdays, Sunday, Monday, 12 p.m., 12 a.m. all the time. The guys that I have now are all project-based. Like Saudi has been in phases, you know, like I have a certain amount of deliverables for phase one, then I finish that. They were like, okay, now we need the next step, you know, your signature experiences and service culture documents, whatever. I'm now doing that. And then I have others which are project. Like I'm working for a women's group that needs a rebranding. So what I've done there is I've looked at a freelancer I know in Los Angeles and she's helping me with the graphic design and I'm doing the strategic part, brand guidelines and so on, and she does the graphic visual ID part. So I could say more projects except for the Sun Secret Collection, which was a year of being fully in-house.

TABARAK: Do you like that mix or are you just now discovering the mix that you like?

ELENA: It's a very good question. I'm probably discovering the mix that I like. I think what I would find the perfect sort of formula would be maybe two clients that kind of take up three, four days a week, and that leaves you maybe with one or two days to be a bit more flexible on projects you're really passionate about that may not have a lot of, you know, budgets or, you know, even helping somebody out who's growing, you know, just doing the funner sort of more, you know, less financially viable stuff.

TABARAK: The one thing I've learned from a lot of freelancers is they like to solve problems. They like to solve problems. And it's like I'm going to find the problems that I like, right? Yeah. The fun ones, right? And sometimes they're challenging but they're still fun, right?

ELENA: Yeah, yeah. I've done a bit of both. I have to admit, like some of the problems I've had to change or to solve have not been exactly my, you know, like I found myself doing like copywriting for websites and stuff, and I'm like I did this like 20 years ago, you know, and I'm like fine, I'll do it again, you know. There's other stuff that you really, really enjoy much more, which of course then is more interesting.

TABARAK: Okay. One last question and then I'll walk you through some of the stuff that I'm seeing out there in the fractional world. But it sounded like you had, okay, so how long have you been doing this now specifically?

ELENA: About a year and a half.

TABARAK: A year and a half. Okay. So you're past what I've been calling the baby fractional phase, the six months, and you kind of figured it out, right? But you're actually very quick compared to a lot of people. You have a lot of skill sets, so you scale pretty quickly. And so now you're in this phase where you're figuring out, you have all your moves, you're putting them all together, right?

ELENA: Yeah, more or less, yes.

TABARAK: Right? And so I guess the question is this, when you're ramped up to your ideal client set of five, right? You're at three, you get to five. If I were to wave a magic wand and you can solve one problem to unlock your business and make it the best thing that you like, what would that be?

ELENA: So depends. You mean for me, like if it was my, so to unlock my business, what would it be?

TABARAK: Yeah, what's one thing that you would, I mean, maybe the thing that's on your head and you said, hey, if I had a magic wand and I can make it disappear or change or do something, how would that make your business great? What would that be?

JACQUI: Right.

TABARAK: Okay. All right. Any thoughts? Feedback? Being in the world for so long and, I mean, obviously, having your background.

JACQUI: So, I mean, I have not been in a situation where I, this is very odd, in the last, well, since 2006, and then, so I spent 2006 to early 2016 building out the fractional thing and then bringing on partners, all that stuff. And in that timeframe, I never once, you're not going to believe me, I never once had to find my next thing.

TABARAK: Yeah.

JACQUI: Not once. My problem was, God, I have to say no. I don't really want to say no. I wish I could say yes. So, one of my solutions was let's bring on another, you know, let's bring on partners. Then I had to go down the whole route of evaluating partners. Like, you know, you bring on a partner, you've got to interview them. There's a whole vetting process, right? I mean, there was all of that. And in an LLP kind of a, you know, format, you know, with the partner agreement and operating agreement, all of that, all of the backend paperwork and stuff. And it was decidedly unfulfilling.

TABARAK: And then you have to manage them.

JACQUI: Well, no, so that's the whole thing about LLPs. You don't manage them, they're not your employees, they're partners. And no one gets a W-2, we all get K-1s. That's what an LLP structure is all about. Like law firms, those partners, those people who are called partners.

TABARAK: Yeah.

JACQUI: Yeah, they get K-1s, they're not W-2ed.

TABARAK: Okay.

JACQUI: It doesn't work like that. The people who are the paralegals and all that, they're W-2ed.

TABARAK: Well, why did you go that approach instead of hiring? Why were you thinking about that approach instead of essentially building out an agency or having your own partners?

JACQUI: The agency model is infinitely broken. The margins are crappy. If you're at 30% margins, you're doing fantastic. And that just ain't my cup of tea. Plus, you work your ass off. You're either having to do client work because someone on your team leaves and you can't find good hires, or you're forever trying to keep your good hires and you're cutting into your margins. No, it's a miserable existence.

TABARAK: Plus just I'll give you a sneak peek into our kind of growth plan, but it's fractionals, fractionals and their clients, agencies, and then agencies from there. And the reality is our step one is know how you do the next thing. And then as you're working through the tasks, we learn how to do the next thing. And then our AI starts automating and taking on tasks for you. And so we move in that direction in those steps. The agency model, we're getting a lot of questions. People keep asking us for that. We're not there yet, but I can see a path to doing something like that. But to your point, agencies aren't unlocked unless you can have that AI doing a lot of the work for you, you know?

JACQUI: Right. I mean, the agency model is also broken, has been broken for years. And now AI is just going to completely force whatever is happening up the old to just completely go away. Because I mean, I came from a traditional agency world. I came from a consultancy that was part of publicists. So, I mean, that is my background in the early parts of my career before I went in-house and worked at a startup and raised a lot of venture money and learned a lot. And then worked for a very large publicly traded software company. You know, all of that. And you can see all of that in LinkedIn. I mean, I have both sides, but I came from an agency, which is a long time ago. And having been in it and then being a client, it is completely broken. It is horrible. Because, you know, it's a complete bait and switch. The people who manage and run your accounts and do the execution are just paid not so great. And they're not very smart. Or if they're smart, they're not very experienced. So, what happens is clients end up training those kids. And I just, I think it's broken. So, I'm not, I won't build an agency. I mean, if you want to call it an AI-first agency for most of the grunt work with principles, now that's a possible. Now, that's an intriguing model.

TABARAK: Built into the system, correct.

JACQUI: Yeah, the principles are number one. They have to be there, right? Well, you have agents. You just have a platform that is all agents that do the tactical execution. The people, the people who are principles, who are 15 years, 20 years, you know, with the experience to provide context and revisions, if you would, to the outputs from the machines.

TABARAK: Correct.

JACQUI: And then they're the ones who, you know, who liaise with clients every day. Now, that also, then your gating factor is truly to be strategic, to be a strategic consultancy, which is what I want to be. Not all, doesn't sound like that is what your target audience is. Mine is always, I prefer to be of counsel, to provide frameworks. And then to, I like to teach my clients how to fish. I don't want to do any of the fishing. Because the fishing is infinitely boring.

TABARAK: Correct.

JACQUI: So, if I can build, if I can provide the frameworks, facilitate the use of those frameworks for a period of time, and then move on to the next thing, to the next client, that is my preferred motion. That is my go-to-market and that's my execution motion. This is not true for what you're building. You're building for companies, firms, solopreneurs, whatever, you know, because it sounds like you have a spectrum.

TABARAK: Yeah.

JACQUI: You're building for those that want recurring revenue and they don't want to have to continue to spend the bulk of their time looking for new clients. They want stable clients and then the ability to add clients every year with some measure of predictability. I mean, that's what you're giving them.

TABARAK: I think that's the core thing. For us, our ideal thing is we don't want to be AI overlords. We want the optionality of choice. Our first positioning would be that stability, but also to give you the option. If you want to have smaller engagements or you want to have longer engagements, it's up to you. You need to suggest how you want to operate. We'll help you get to that point. But the reality is, yes, stability is what everyone is asking for right now. I think that's the core thing that people are significantly trying to get right now.

JACQUI: Right. Yeah. Right. Like I said, I'm an outlier because I didn't have ambitions ever of growing to a national global force. That was not my thing. I found fulfillment and satisfaction in taking a company from this level to what we all know from an investment perspective to the next stage so that they can get the valuation that they deserve.

TABARAK: For sure.

JACQUI: That they worked for. So that was my goal. It's a very different marketing and it's a very different goal than a pure marketing goal.

TABARAK: Yeah.

JACQUI: Pure marketing goal.

TABARAK: And the thing is, were all your clients referrals? You said you have no trouble getting to the next person.

JACQUI: No, I have never run a single ad in 20 years. I mean, I have become to be known as the fixer. Like, you know, an angel says, can you talk to this founder for me? Because something's not working. And I would have lunch with the investor. I go, OK, so what's the problem? Like, candidly, you know, off the record, what's the issue? And they'll tell me and then they'll ask me to make my own evaluation. And then, you know, and of course, we would have sidebar conversations. I mean, it's a very unfair thing because I'm always brought in as the villain because I'm usually referred in. And so, you know, my first 30 days is usually evaluating and also building rapport to ensure that they didn't think of me as the enemy because I'm not going to get anywhere and help them if they think of me as the enemy. So that's a very, very different thing than than, you know, a tech company, a tech startup. So, by the way, I spent the last six, seven years, like right before COVID, working with PE-owned and PE-backed companies. Now, that's a whole other ball of wax. And that is all about.

TABARAK: PE or startups?

JACQUI: Oh, I hate PE. I hate them. I mean, no, I don't work for PE. I work for their companies. But effectively, I'm at the board meetings and I can't stand them. I just can't stand the way they think. I don't like their priorities. I don't like their value system. It is completely. I see PE as the main villain in the affordable housing shortage in this country. You can probably tell my politics. I see them as the biggest, most horrible, the driving force behind the lack of affordable housing and how young people are priced out of markets for their first homes. They're the villain. And it's actually not even both Republicans and Democrats are seeing it now. And there's actually action in different states in state legislation. Bills are going through them to stop like one corporate owner from buying up X number of homes in certain like in, you know, in a certain demographic.

TABARAK: In a DMA.

JACQUI: Like a DMA. So, I mean, it's now become a recognized nonpartisan problem. It's a social problem. So, anyway.

TABARAK: I see we're running across our time right now. I really appreciate the time, Jackie, so much.

JACQUI: No worries.

TABARAK: You gave me a lot of insight. I know we're not the right exact fit, but thank you so much again. I really do appreciate it. And if you need anything or, I mean, if you don't mind, I'll reach out to you. I mean, I love your insight. What are you working on next? Do you have another project?

JACQUI: I'm actually in the. So, I'm also an angel investor. That's the other thing that I don't put on my LinkedIn.

TABARAK: Yeah.

JACQUI: For obvious reasons. I don't like to be pitched. So, at least I don't think I have it on my LinkedIn. I'm going to have to recheck now that you've asked. Now you got me paranoid. So, I'm actually working on some business plans. I'm looking, I'm evaluating some opportunities, some investment opportunities. So, that's part of my day as well. I'm actually actively, I was doing that right before this meeting.

TABARAK: So, I'll bring this. I'm going to keep on talking to a lot of fractionals. I think we're going to move in a certain direction. If I get an aha moment, would you mind if I ever reached out for any advice at any point later on?

JACQUI: Yes. I'll just tell you that I'm probably going to be more useful to you as, not as like your customer persona. But if you all are building this thing out and you're looking and you eventually are going to be taking capital, not like an SBA loan. But some sort of equity type based capital. I'm going to, you know, I'm happy to provide that kind of feedback.

TABARAK: I'd love that. I would, I'd be happy to reach back out.

JACQUI: Okay. Where are you based?

TABARAK: I'm actually, so we're originally based at Seattle, but we're based out of Bangkok, Thailand right now.

JACQUI: Ah, got it.

TABARAK: Yeah. So, we're building and taking care of our burn rate while we're out here.

JACQUI: Yeah, right. I mean, it's, it's kind of nuts what the economics are. So, good for you.

TABARAK: Thank you. I appreciate it. Thank you again, Jackie. Talk to you later.

JACQUI: Okay, bye-bye.

TABARAK: Bye-bye.

If you got leads, you got CPA type people that don't know fractionals, the messaging was wrong, right? And your offer was wrong, the hook was wrong. If you find the right person that knows how to do it, I'd pay them, bring them in on a trial and be like, hey, I just need you to try this out. And they need to know, they need to be talking to ICPs. They need to be interviewing you to figure out, just like I did, it's like, what is that problem? What is that moment? So that they can get people, because the reality is if people are coming in and talking to you and they're the wrong people, it worked.

JASON: I worked my way up to CFO at a credit union here in Orange County. That credit union was absorbed into a larger credit union, and of course, that CFO got to keep his job. So I started doing some consulting really just to fill in the gap.
My intention was to go back and to find another job. But as I was doing the consulting, I sat down with the gentleman just doing some networking. He said, yeah, I'm a fractional CFO, I have four clients, they pay me $5,000 a month each, and I spend about a week on each of their businesses.
So that was a little over 10 years ago. That was the first time I'd ever heard the term fractional CFO. And I thought, that sounds like a pretty sweet gig.
So I've been kind of doing consulting slash fractional CFO work depending on what the project requires since. And up to this point, I've been relying on recruiters or maybe larger firms who go out and get the business and then contract out to people like me. I found out that one of the firms that I work with keeps, I mean, they keep the lion's share of the fee.
Well, I'll go out and develop the business and just keep that fee for myself. So now I'm starting, yeah, so I'm at the point to where I'm starting to rebuild my client base. So I was, like five or six clients is probably a comfortable capacity for me.
Given what's going on with AI and all the tools that are being developed, I'd be really curious to see if that doesn't change.

TABARAK: That's a big thing that we've been talking about, yeah. I guess the question for you right now, do you have a mix for your client base? You said five or six, is that five full time, some projects, like how do you mix that?

And so I, it's typically I try, I target about 20 hours a month, 25 hours a month for each. So that's why five or six makes a good, like I said, it's a comfortable capacity.
I could probably take on more, but it would get a little tighter.

TABARAK: What's the most you've ever done?

The other one, I basically just collected it. So I wasn't complaining. So yeah, I think, so my goal is to get a backup to like, say, four or five clients where I've got a comfortable income and then start developing my own firm.

TABARAK: I love that.

So that, yeah.

So I said, okay, fine. It's okay. You know, I mean, it's, I've gotten one person who is really interested and had great phone calls with them and then he just ghosted me.

And at the end of the day, when it was all said and done, I put an extra $25,000 of revenue on it.

JASON: A month. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So I'm confident, and I'm going to pay for myself many times over.

Essentially what it is, it's a command center for fractions. All of your work from different systems, like Notion, from the spreadsheet that you have, whatever it is, wherever you have work, we have it all come into one place, right? TABARAK: We started with Eisenhower, but utilizing Eisenhower and AI is not consistent. The AI just hallucinates, so your task can change every single time, right? What we did was actually have to build out a formula, and that formula takes in your goals, and then within your goals, the deadline, how important it is, when you tie it to a stakeholder, how much revenue does your client have? KATHRYN: Sure. So a little bit about myself. So my career has been in brand marketing. I started in brand marketing as an assistant brand manager out of school, working for Newell Brands. I was working in power tool accessories. So on the professional side of the business, I was shadowing contractors, HVAC techs, plumbers, electricians on job sites. Super fun. Tectonic plates kind of shifted at the company. I ended up moving up to Vermont of all places with the Green Mountain Coffee Roasters, which is now Keurig Dr. Pepper. So I worked on a variety of brands there, everything from brands that we owned. So when I was there, we owned about 90% of the brands that would go through the Keurig system in some sort of capacity, whether they were brands that we owned, brands that we licensed out. I worked on a brand called Swiss Miss where we licensed their likeness, matched their formula in a K-cup pod, and then we sold it and gave them a royalty. So I managed that relationship and then worked on other types of relationships that were essentially co-manufactured products for Starbucks, Dunkin' Donuts, Folgers, a variety of other brands. So I probably worked on over 20 brands around the coffee, tea, and cocoa space while I was there. Tectonic plates shifted again. People were moving everywhere. They were shutting down different brands. And so I had the opportunity to end up in Southern Vermont. My husband had gotten his dream job in Southern Vermont. He's a big fly fisherman. And so he got a job, he's an engineer, designing fly rods and reels for the Orvis company. So he was pretty excited about that. And I was like, What in God's name am I going to do in Southern Vermont for, you know, in CPG? So I ended up finding a brand called New Chapter, which is a subsidiary of P&G. And so I worked for that brand for the last 10 years. It was essentially founder-led. P&G acquired it about 4 years prior to me joining. And so it was going through that transition of being founder-led to being integrated into the P&G system. And, you know, it's obviously bumpy when that stuff happens. So historically, marketing was a service agency. What I did there was I made marketing and brand the center of the company and so did a lot of transformational change. And it was essentially me and one other person. And then I built it up, created a brand team, created an in-house media team, created an in-house social team, creative team. I ended up taking over sustainability in the last couple of years, customer service, education, I mean, you name it. So it essentially, you know, built this organization up, really got back down to fundamentals, right? You know, we were, we tried to love all of our children equally. We had over 300 SKUs in the portfolio. And it was just, you know, peanut butter spreading. We weren't making choices on the products that we were supporting, the customers that we were focusing on. And so it was a lot of foundational, you know, strategic work around what are the products that we're choosing? Who are we choosing to focus on? And then also the consumer, right? And so a lot of strategic choice around who we were going to design for, how we were going to, you know, bring that brand strategy to life. And so spent a lot of time doing that and, you know, grew the business pretty tremendously over the last couple of years. And then in December, I decided to take the leap. So this is our approach for solving this problem right now. So it's kind of like Google Maps for your work week. Our tool is essentially a system that's going to help you find the most perfect thing to do next. Right. And this is based on your goals, based on key stakeholders. And what we call key stakeholders is it's your distribution pipeline. It's client A, it's project B. Right. Each of these are essentially you get to outline what they are. You get to outline your goals. And our system, essentially, it's going to be your command center. It pulls information from everything. And then what it does, it prioritizes and scores all of them. And so every day when you start your day, essentially what you can do is we can do your reprioritization button on there. You tell it what's changed. It understands what's changed, what new things came in, what emails came in, what calendar invites came in. And then it can rescore everything so that you're always doing the right thing next. Right. So the purpose of it is what we're seeing is it really does help for the people that are approaching their capacity with a lot of clients. A lot of business and a lot of things. And then we're also seeing now, you're probably our third baby fractional and you're probably the youngest one at four months, but we have one at six and eight months now. And now they're seeing that, OK, we have one solid client. Now we want to bring in a couple of other people. And now they're seeing this as a structure for setting up their business. Right. Because now there's no, they're coming from a different world. Right. And so here's your, why don't you, what do you think about this? Do you have any feedback on it? Do you see any possibility?

KATHRYN: No, I think it makes sense. I think it makes sense because I can see how it could be a constant juggling. Right. Because you've got multiple different clients, multiple different projects that you need to somehow prioritize and sort them out. And you're looking at different systems. Right. You know, so I think, I think it makes sense. I think it makes a lot of sense. I think the way that you guys approach this and trying to figure out, like, what are the challenges? Like, OK, what do we want to do about it? And then how can we add value? Makes a lot of sense.

Our next thing is then we will be incorporating playbooks. Right. So then your systems in there. And then the AI will then start operating on that. And the first playbook we're working on is solving for the feast of famine cycle. Right. So.

KATHRYN: Interesting. So that would be the next thing. And that coming from my sales background is it's helping with ICP, helping with your packaging, with your promise, what your offer is. Right. Back into what is your math. How many people do you need to talk to each month? Right. And then our system will be able to flag that and know how much time you're spending versus on, you know, on X work versus internal work versus prospecting and such.

TABARAK: Yeah. Makes sense. Makes sense. Oh, this is awesome. Well, way to go. You guys are, like, it's amazing. Like, good for you.

KATHRYN: Thank you. Thank you. It's been good and bad. Good and bad. I appreciate that. Yeah. I honestly, I don't know. I'll be honest. I don't know if we're in the right spot as in overlap. For problem solution. Yeah, I'm not sure. I guess I'm so new with this at this point.

TABARAK: A hundred percent. But you have my contact. If you have any ideas or questions or anything, I've talked to fractions all day long. So you've got a lifeline through me. If you need, if you have any questions, I appreciate it. Thank you. And then whenever we get to getting the feast or famine cycle stuff figured out, we'll get close to it. I'd love to reach back out and that would be amazing.

KATHRYN: So for sure. That would be great. Well, thank you so much. I really appreciate it. Have a good rest of the evening.

TABARAK: You too. Take care. Bye bye.

LAILA: Then I went in-house at a job board monster that is declining very badly and I lost my job there last year, which wasn't unexpected. The job market in the UK was pretty terrible, which was why I ended up staying because I figured it would be better to leave with some money than to kind of just get kicked out with nothing. And really that kind of varied career, while it is a strength, what companies in the UK are looking for at the moment, which I've never really needed, wanted before in marketing.
In marketing, your skillset has always been marketing and quite often the industry has been nice to have, you know, because you can learn another industry unless it's like a heavily regulated industry or something like manufacturing, you know, where there's a real need. But if it's professional services, you've quite often been able to move around and that just isn't the case anymore. Now they want people with 20 years experience and because there's so many people looking for jobs and not enough jobs and companies can afford to be picky.
So that was what really started me thinking I needed to be looking wider than just looking for a job. So I started on this journey. I kind of came up with an idea for a report to write about the challenges facing job boards and convinced someone I'd been working with, I'd been writing a couple of articles for it and I convinced them to pay me some money to do that.
So that launched and then I, through a friend, I was introduced to someone else and I got my first kind of fractional CMO gig through them. It's not paying loads of money at the moment, but it got me, basically, it's got me into work. I've been doing a lot of work and it's a lot easier to win clients when you have things on your LinkedIn that you can say that you're doing.
But it's really helped me develop what my practice is, you know, what do I, what am I doing? And for me, there's been, I'm in the transition from someone who has always worked in a job and had structure set for me by having too many meetings in a job to now having to be someone who is having to work out that if I'm going to someone and saying, what are you buying? You know, give me some money. I need some money. I'd love to work for you.
Give me some money. What are they buying? You know, and then how am I then delivering that as productively as possible? That I can maximize my income and minimize my time. And I want this to be a lifestyle business.
I love working. The other problem I've got, so I think the problem that the big problem I've got is I love what I do. This is the first time I've loved my job as much as this.
I'm getting to do strategy and I've realized I should have been a strategist my whole career. But I'm also very commercial and I'm good with an account management, blah, blah, blah. So, you know, that's fine.
But actually the stuff that I'm good at, what I'm not good at or what I feel very uncomfortable with, I think I'm too British for it, is going and introducing myself to people and kind of working out if there's work to be done, like the new business and how do I go? And I hadn't really taken care of my network. When you work in agency land, certainly in the UK, you don't really need to, right? Or you haven't really needed to. The agency you work for, the clients you worked on, the work was visible to everyone.
You could say that these were the campaigns that you did. So I've never really needed to do networking. So I've kind of, I neglected that.
I hadn't been on LinkedIn for years until I got made redundant. So I think for me, that's one of the challenges. And I think it's, how someone has described it to me is, you know, it's the difference between working on your business or working in your business.
And when you're delivering for clients, it's the being in the business, you're doing the work. And it's very easy when there's a load to do, to put off the things that are creating new business. And then the other challenge for me really is just working out efficiency and effectiveness, right? So how do I get to the point I want to get to with the client as quickly as possible so I can make more money? So I can spend more time prospecting or meeting new people and still give them what they need.
And it's that, it's that difference between being in a job where your job is to deliver, whereas now 40, 30 to 40% of my job needs to be trying to win new clients.

TABARAK: First off, I appreciate you talking with me, and how should I call you, is it Marugapod?

MARUGAPOD: Yep, you got it, I'm impressed.

TABARAK: Did I really do it right?

MARUGAPOD: Yes.

TABARAK: So yeah, like I was saying in the message, we're building a product for fractional operators.

MARUGAPOD: Okay.

TABARAK: And just a little background about myself, so I worked at DocuSign for about 13 years in a consulting role.

MARUGAPOD: Okay.

TABARAK: And when I left about a year ago, I decided to move to Bangkok, so I'm actually halfway across the world right now.

MARUGAPOD: No kidding.

TABARAK: And create a startup, but one of the first things I got into was consulting work.

MARUGAPOD: Okay.

TABARAK: And what ended up happening was, I took on a few clients, but I ended up being a lot more hands-on than I intended. And it stopped being an advisory role, and I was really acting as one of their employees.

MARUGAPOD: Okay.

TABARAK: And I started getting more into fractional work.

MARUGAPOD: Okay.

TABARAK: And right when I got up to about three clients, what I found was, I was having a really hard time juggling my own time versus my clients' time. And I was kind of suffering on my own schedule. And that's kind of what created the product that I wanted to talk to you about today.

MARUGAPOD: Okay.

TABARAK: All right.

TABARAK: Yeah, yeah. I appreciate it. MARUGAPOD: Oh, wow. Okay. That's all right. That's good. That's good. All right. All right, Brian. Good luck to you. Okay. Keep building it.
