MICHAEL: So I'm near outside of Philadelphia, in Jersey.

TABARAK: Okay, okay. It's been a while since I've been out there. It's funny, I have been out of, well, last time I was in the States was in March last year.
But prior to that, I mean, I grew up in Seattle, Washington. And the last place I worked at, I was at DocuSign. I was a regional vice president, been doing sales for like 20 plus years.
And then in that first round of layoffs, I got let go. And I finally got the golden handcuffs to let go.
And I said, I'll take some time off and go travel the world.
Come to find out, I love to work. I can never be that world traveler forever. So I decided I wanted to start a business, convince one of my buddies who was a director at DocuSign for us to start learning marketing, figure out, since we've done sales, we weren't really versed on the marketing side of it.
And we knew that'd be very important. So we started doing that on our path to becoming founders. And naturally we got into consulting to make some money on the side and do fractional work.
And what we realized and what we found was while trying to start our business, we were juggling our business plus all of our clients. And it was just, it was a headache. We said, this is wild.
And on top of that, we were in these crossroads where we had to maintain getting more clients. And so there's this feast or famine on this side and then pushing off business goals on the other side. And so it was a pretty big insight for us.
And then we realized, well, maybe there's something here. Maybe we're biased. So let's talk to as many fractionals as possible.
A few dozen fractionals deep now in our conversations. And we ran into a lot of different problems. So that's kind of why I got you on the line today is to give you an overview of what we're kind of dealing with.
See if there's any problems that you're dealing with as a fractional yourself. Be very happy to share with you all the problems we're seeing out in the market and how people are solving for that. And then ultimately we have an approach that we think may be a solution for this problem.
And I'd love to kind of tackle that, go over that with you. If it sounds like it's good, it's a good fit.
Maybe I can set up some time to show you the full demonstration of the product, but I didn't want to waste the entire call today so that there wasn't any alignment at all. But that's kind of what I have. I'd love to hear a little bit about you and kind of your background as well.

MICHAEL: Sure. So I've spent my life doing marketing, sales enablement, partner ecosystems, et cetera, in the B2B technology space mostly. When COVID hit, I decided that I was going to need to find a way to adjust to that reality and started doing this on a fractional basis, which was actually much more successful than I think I ever thought it was going to be at that point when I first put that out there.
So I mostly work with founders and smaller firms who don't have a dedicated marketing resource where marketing is founder led. There may be a marketing team, there may be a marketing motion, but they're trying to say, how do I take this idea that is currently me and my network and scale it into a business that grows and repeats? The specific nature of that challenge is different nearly every time, which my ADHD brain thinks is awesome. And I am really excited to find the key that opens the lock.
So I would take someone in your position and say, here's what I see in your go-to-market strategy. Here's where I see opportunities for you to grow and scale differently than you are today. Here's what I would recommend changing, et cetera.
And that's generally the work that I do. So in setting this up, I did a look at Vantam and approached this that way. It doesn't mean that it's not a tool I'm interested in.
It's just the way I set up for these conversations.

TABARAK: 100%, I appreciate that. So I'd ask you this, what would you say your specialty is in the—

MICHAEL: I think the challenge is that my specialty is really finding what your organization needs and what makes it tick.
So I was approached a couple of years ago by a competitive intelligence consulting agency that said, you know, our bread and butter has been life sciences, but our life sciences work is down and we want you to come in and build our website better for life sciences. And I came back to them and said, no, you don't. And I'll tell you why.
And I said, here are your website. Here is your competitors websites. The people you told me are most important to you.
And all of you collectively get roughly 1% of your traffic from life sciences from organic search. So no one in organic search is saying who is the best life sciences consultant, competitive intelligence consultant. They're saying I'm going directly to company X, Y, or Z that I've already heard of.
So I'm like, what you need to do is a brand awareness play. And here's how I'd established that. But the fact that I moved it to them with competitive intelligence, which is their thing, but sort of made their eyes pop.
And as of 2025 was their best year ever in life sciences and they're, you know, doing gangbusters. So it was a change in strategy and we still did some SEO work, but I was like, you know, here's how we're going to transform this upcoming event where it's a small event, but everyone at that event is who you want to sell to. So here's how we're going to transform that conversation.
I did a giveaway for them there that they were, they were concerned about. They're actually calling me from the event. It was a bottle of bourbon.
They're from Kentucky. And I'm like, put a nice bottle bourbon up on the account. And they're like, why? And I'm like, because you tell me everyone there is talking about bourbon.
Let's not make this harder than it needs to be. And then it won't be like the first night. It's like, everyone's coming over and going, Hey, what'd you do with that bottle of Blanton's there? And we're like, Oh, we're doing a giveaway and turned into a conversation.
The guy that bought the bourbon has personally, his company has personally spent over $2 million with them since doing so. Um, and you know, it just, it was the icebreaker that got the conversation started and then rolled them into the conversation they wanted to have. Then we turned that into a survey of the life sciences competitive intelligence community that they do annually, which is now a district.
It's this, the survey is a lead. Uh, entering the survey is a lead magnet. Downloading the survey is a lead magnet.
They're setting up every time it comes out, all the top farmers are like, this is good. Can you show us the data behind the data privately? And we have a sense of all sorts of meetings and they're just there. They're expanding rapidly in the space because the survey is actually an opportunity, not just to have this cool data, but it shows their expertise.
Here's how we gather data, analyze the data and present the data in useful ways. So that's an example of, this is not a typical marketing motion for most organizations, but it's what their market demanded at the point in time that they were. And other people, I look at it and say, you know, uh, how, how are you going to, uh, you know, you don't seem to have at, um, Vantom as far as I've seen a, a, a sort of inbound model, a way for people to get involved in the product without speaking to you or, you know, and, and, you know, then setting up campaigns and processes that drive people to that.
You have either people that download, try and buy and never talk to you, which is great. Or you're getting the people that have, you know, uh, more serious questions are farther through the funnel. So those are the types of it's, it's, to me, it's cracking the code on go to market.

# Phil Problem

---

TABARAK: Hey, Phil, how's it going?

PHIL: Hello, good morning, or good evening, every how it is at your house.

TABARAK: Yeah, I'm in Bangkok, Thailand, so it is actually 10 p.m. right now.

PHIL: Okay, good, I've been to Bangkok a couple of times in my life.

TABARAK: How did you like that?

PHIL: Oh, I liked it, I've been on business as well as on a pleasure trip, so it was good. Yeah. Yeah.

TABARAK: Yeah, I found my way here. Actually, give you a little bit of background. My last adult job was over at DocuSign. I was a regional vice president for sales, been doing it for like 20 years, and in November 2022, I got let go in the first round of layoffs.

PHIL: Wow.

TABARAK: I had been wanting to leave, but I had the golden handcuffs, and it was the perfect timing. I took on a new role, new position. When I switched to that position, honestly, I didn't need to be there. It was perfect for me to leave, so I got my severance package, I got my stock, they gave me my laptop, there was nothing bad, and then I went and traveled the world. I went to Europe, Africa, Southeast Asia, ended up doing two years jaunting around, ended up in Bangkok and Thailand in general, and fell in love with it, but realized I love to work. I can't do this forever. I thought I could. I thought I wanted a vacation, but that was a long time, so I ended up deciding, hey, I wanna do my own thing. I'm gonna start my business, connected with a buddy of mine that was at DocuSign. Him and I both realized we started business before, but we didn't really have the marketing side of the house, so we knew sales. He's a director of solutions engineering, so we actually spent a year across the world learning marketing together. He worked on his time, I was on my time. We used a lot of different tools to work async, to work together. I convinced him to leave DocuSign, left a 400K a year job, came over here, brought his wife, and we started his business. While we're figuring out the exact product we were gonna build, we decided, hey, let's start doing some consulting to get some gigs in the meantime. Did consulting, and then it turned more into fractional rather than just advisory stuff. And then we got into that problem where we're trying to grow our business, but we have client deliverables, and we just gotta get more clients, and now we're juggling two things. And then we realized, well, maybe this is the business. There's a big problem here we're trying to solve. And so that led us on to what we're doing now. So we started reaching out to dozens, I think we're almost about 50 fractionals we've spoken to so far. We wanted to essentially kind of suss out the shape of this problem set. Are other fractionals dealing with this? What are the problems they're dealing with? Essentially, that's what got us to you is really wanna understand kind of like some of the, in this call today, what problems you might be dealing with. I can go over the problems that I'm seeing other fractionals are dealing with now, present some of the solutions that they've been using, and then kind of go over our approach. We have a product in development right now. We have an overall idea that I think might solve for this problem. Our hope is that at the end of this call, maybe we find a good problem fit. And if that's the case, and you find our approach being very interesting, we could set up some further time later on to show you the product. And from there, if you like the product, you saw some real value in it, we're actually having about 10 to 12 fractionals we're putting into our customer development program. So we're essentially gonna give them the product for about eight, 10 weeks, and then we build the rest of the product all the way to their configuration. Because I think our core problem we're trying to solve, we want that real response rate and real feedback from them. But how does that sound? Does that sound okay for today in this call?

PHIL: Yeah, yeah, sounds good.

TABARAK: Why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself, Phil? What brought you to where you're at now? Tell me your history. What are you up to nowadays?

PHIL: Yeah, I'm kind of in a hybrid fractional dancing back and forth between COO and CFO because I've had both those positions I've also been an IT director before, but I don't advertise that too much. So I don't like to get too much into IT projects. But it's a good background to have, right? Yeah. So I grew up in manufacturing and distribution in the bulk of my career, engineer with an MBA. And I eventually started running those companies. That's the COO side of me. And then I've also had a full-time real estate house flipping firm way back when. And when all that crashed, a friend of mine was starting a healthcare business. He said, why don't you come join me, right? I said, okay. And so I was the fifth employee and a partner in the business. And we grew that to over a hundred employees, multiple locations around Southeastern USA. And that became an Inc 5,000 company year over year. I eventually sold those shares back to the company and went out and started just doing consulting work. And that led me into some business coaching. And then a friend of mine from kindergarten, we grew up together. He said, hey, similar to you, he got let go from a big company, primarily because he kept outselling the quota and he was making too much money for them, which is ridiculous. That's what you want your salespeople to do, right? But anyway. He said, hey, we've been doing this for other people so long, why don't we do it for ourselves? I said, okay, I'm game. So we started a healthcare practice here in Atlanta and grew that, a men's clinic that grew to about $4 million very quickly. We shut that down toward the end of COVID for a number of reasons, but we've started another health clinic dealing with knees, osteoarthritis of the knees. And we just exited that company last April, right out of Europe. Yeah, yeah. And so I went back and started doing business coaching again and that turns into a fractional COO slash CFO type assignments. And just to tell you more about that, I have a lot of business coaching clients and I have a couple of fractional clients, but that's the key for fractionals. We don't need a thousand, we only need a hundred. We need four, eight. It's hard to handle eight, right?

TABARAK: Yeah, yeah.

PHIL: And I kind of picture it in buckets of, I try to dedicate one week a month type to each fractional type assignment. So for me, I think four is plenty, but the problem is you don't know when one or two of those are gonna drop off, right? So you're back to surviving for a little bit till you find two more. So finding a way to be active and being able to deliver the service is one thing. The second one is kind of always be prospecting for that other one or two that you can add on. ## Phillip Probelm.md

# Phillip Probelm

*Source text file:* `originals_txt/Phillip Probelm.txt`

*Speaker turns were separated using **`anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6`** via OpenRouter. Verify against the recording if a line must be exact.*

*Plain transcript: original had no per-line speaker names.*

---

PHILLIP: Hey, how's it going? Can you hear me? Hello, can you hear me? Philip?

TABARAK: Yeah, I can now. Are you able to hear me?

PHILLIP: Yeah, I can hear you perfectly. Okay, great.

TABARAK: Yeah, how are you doing, man?

PHILLIP: I'm doing great, man. Thank you for taking the time to set this up.

TABARAK: Absolutely, absolutely.
I appreciate it. Are you in a sound room? What is that back there?

PHILLIP: Nah, man, it's actually a dining room, man. It's just decorated, man.

TABARAK: It's a really nice design, man. I thought you were in the studio.

PHILLIP: Nah, I appreciate it.
Yeah, folks get carried away here with designing. We moved here and it was empty and all that good stuff. And so, like I said, now, you know, ladies got it, getting there, looking pretty and all that good stuff.

TABARAK: Yeah, where are you based out of?

PHILLIP: I'm in Katy, Texas. So it's like a suburb right outside of Houston.

TABARAK: Okay, that's awesome.
That's awesome. You said you're in Bangkok, right?

PHILLIP: I'm in Bangkok, yeah. Yeah, we brought you out there.

TABARAK: Oh, man, I guess I can kind of give you a little background around me. My name is Tabarik. I did about 20 years in sales.
I was at Verizon for the longest. And then I ended up at DocuSign and worked my way up through the ranks there. I was a regional vice president.
And in those first round of layoffs back in 2022, I got let go. I got promoted into a position with only two reps and my position was gone, right? Which was great. I had the golden handcuffs.
I've been meaning to leave and I had no reason to. So because it was an early layoff, they gave me a great severance. I got all my stock.
I got my laptop. Everything was great. I had no excuse to not travel the world.
So I spent the next two years traveling Europe, Dubai, and then Southeast Asia. And me and my best friend ended up coming out here and working. He was working.
He ended up going back. I stayed. And after I did all my shenanigans and just living life as a traveler, I realized that that's just not my life forever.
I love to work. There's part of me that always has to be doing something challenging. And I just found that my ultimate purpose wasn't going to be that.
So I ended up connecting with another buddy of mine from DocuSign for about a year. Even though I was still gallivanting around Southeast Asia, we realized we both needed to learn marketing, right? I was in sales forever. He was a director of solution engineering.
He had done some startups. I had done some startups. We realized the biggest gap in today's learning how to do marketing and the go to market and all of that.
If you can be a great salesperson, but you don't understand where the leads come from and how that happens, it doesn't really matter, right? So we were obviously trying to build our own business, but naturally we got into consulting, right? We have many years in one good skill set or a few different skill sets. And so we started working with some startups, helping them go to market with sales, finding their personas, all of that stuff. And then realizing we're not just advising, we're in the trenches, we're doing fractional work, right? And then while we're trying to do fractional work, we're trying to run a business and launch a business.
And as we got to three, four, five clients and just realized that we just couldn't manage this time, there's just this weird way of having, how do you have these competing things? And the ultimate thing is that your business is important. Like you got to put your mask on first, right? But it's very easy to land into this world where you're putting out fires, right? And then slowly your clients will disappear and then you have your business, but you just got to make some more money. And so what happens is we got this insight and we found this insight that fractionals have a key problem.
There's a variety of different problems. So we decided that we wanted to solve that part of that business, right? And bring some of our expertise in sales and marketing and agile and how we build our business and how to work together. For a year, we worked on opposite ends from Seattle and Bangkok.
And so we had to learn how to run a system of accountability, using a combine, using agile, essentially doing things to get done from A to Z, right? And so what happened was I ended up talking to a whole bunch of fractionals and we were compiling this stuff and we're getting to this point now where we have an MVP, we're going to launch, but we want to understand, is it a bias? Is there something that we're learning? Is there problems that we have? So we essentially created a list of problems. We're asking a lot of fractionals and then we're presenting what these problems look like, get some feedback from them. And if that sounds like it's a good idea and you align with a lot of those problems, you see that, then there potentially could be next steps where I can show you the product that we're working on, right? That might be able to help you out.
And if it's not, I mean, I appreciate the time of just learning what I could do for you, you know, and all the lessons that I'm learning from other fractionals and share that with you ultimately. But I guess a question for me to you is like, A, how long have you been in the game? How long have you been doing fractional work?

PHILLIP: So I've been doing fractional since 2022, 20, yeah, about 2022.
So kind of similar to you, man.
I was, I started out of college at a operations leadership program at Geico car insurance company, worked at the corporate office, started entry-level, worked my way up to senior, senior leadership, ended up running a leadership program, again, dream job at that time, responsible for internships, leadership all the way up from, you know, entry-level leadership to all the way to senior directors. And then did some, had some regional aspects and then did some work again, nationwide. And so kind of COVID came, layout reduction in force happened, job got centralized to DC.
I was like, not moving to DC, was offered a role again that I wasn't just, just didn't get me excited. So I decided to leave out, started a raw dog food company, scaled that business for four years, made an exit, went back into corporate medical research consulting, and then did some fractional work with them as well, operations, talent, organizational development, and then became a father, man. And so once I became a father, I was just like, man, I'm out this corporate life.
I don't want to be in the office till 11. So now I'm a stay at home dad while lady, she just left for work. She's a teacher.

TABARAK: Congratulations, man.

TABARAK: Yeah. It's funny because it sounded like people that are in that really good, I got my boundaries, I got the perfect amount of work, they figured it out.

Like how many people you want, how long in that, how long, how did you figure out that, that, that part?

PHILLIP: I think for me, it was lifestyle. A lot of folks, they go business goals first.
And based off of business goals, they identify this is what I need to make. This is how many clients I need to have. For me, I did it a little bit backwards with the reverse engineering, or I guess added a different step.
It was looking at life goals and where I'm currently at and what I'm okay with and what I'm not.
And so the big factor was the daughter, right? Having that daughter, that's something that changes like, okay, I want to be able to spend time with her. I want to be able to close the laptop.
I want to be able to exercise and get in better shape. And that's I did was able to lose like about 75 pounds. I'm in the best shape since I play football right now.
And so it was like figuring out life goals first. And so once I figured out life goals, I aligned that and built the time out for that. And then I looked at the business goals and said, okay, how can I make my business goals fit within this allotment of time? Once I did that, it was like, okay, you start understanding this is what, how many clients I need to have.
If I go quarterly, how many I need to have, if I go six months, if I go nine months as such. And so by building that out, I knew exactly what I was aiming for. And so that helps you again, narrow down your ICP, right? If I have a client that has a ton of structure, they just kind of need you to solve one problem.
That's likely going to get you a quarterly retainer. If you have a client who has a major problem that's impacting their business, they don't have the staff and the tools to get it. That is probably going to have a longer engagement because you're filling in, you're creating what they don't have.
And so those are the folks that I typically look to engage with whenever I'm having those conversations about my services or coming in as a fractional COO is not necessarily just coming in to take on that one project, but it's like, okay, you're lacking, you need the experience, you need the talent, then you also need the processes and the system. So that gives me that, okay, we can have a six month or nine month retainer because it actually makes sense.

TABARAK: That's cool.
Like, did you stumble upon finding that? Were you lucky getting one person? You're like, oh, this is the thing that I got to keep doing. Or like, how did you backwards engineer that ICP? Obviously from your family and life goals, but like, did you know that was the ICP you were looking for?

PHILLIP: No, man. So at first I was just taking on anybody that would that would give me money to fix a problem.
And so that's like early on 2022. But I mean, it was just something that I kind of taught us and ingrained in us at Geico is like how to run towards the problem. And a lot of folks is when you run towards a problem, typically you like folks are saying, okay, this is broken, this is broken, this is broken.
They taught us find the problem and figure out what's causing that and backtrack it to the beginning of where you are. And so reverse engineering. And so once I did that, I was like, okay, I'm going about this the wrong way.
I'm taking on projects for two weeks. I get a little check here or a month or this and that. And it's like, it's not working out.
It's very sporadic. I'm literally in that feast or famine cycle. And it was frustrating.
But it was like, okay, let me, these are the folks that I like to work with. This is the problems that I solve. This is what they look like that are going to be able to give me that, excuse me, that six month retainer or nine month retainer.
And it's literally focusing on those. And so when we have conversations, I might say, hey, to Bark, what are your, what are your, you know, kind of what's going on within your business. And we talk about that.
And you might say, hey, you know what? We're just trying to get through this one sprint and it's just not working. And I can offer a solution. I know folks who do take projects like that.
So I'd recommend that to you. That's not even something that I'm really just going to dive into. But if you're saying, hey, you know what? We've been struggling with this for some time.
It's impacting our profitability. We don't have the resources. We don't have the talent.
We don't have all this. It's like, okay, now this is more aligned with my ICP. And that's the person that I should probably target.

TABARAK: That's pretty cool, man. So if you were to teach that to a new fractional, you'd be like, know who you are and what you want. Understand how much time you want to operate in.

So it's just breaking all these things down and figuring out like using your data, using your abilities, tracking like a big data nerd. So I track everything that I do within my business. So it helps me to be able to understand this is what we need to do in order to get where we want to go.

TABARAK: We're right on this minute. I don't know if you have a meeting right after here.

PHILLIP: No, you're good. You're good.

PHILLIP: I think it's both, man.

And that's just, that's simply not how business works, right? That hasn't happened to me in the time that I've been doing it.
And so it's a little bit of combination of both. One thing at Geico that they did for us whenever I was in that operations leadership program is they took you through all the different departments, right? So you got to go through underwriting, you got to go through sales, you got to go through operations, you got to get a little hint at marketing. And so you see all these different departments that culminate to make the whole entire business function and you get to learn those aspects.

This is how they identify who is really good customers for them versus staying away from folks that aren't really good customers and maybe a little bit more risky. And so it's the same thing with your business.
I think a lot of folks as well, they don't know who their ICP is. They'll say, you know what? I help founders. They have these headlines.
I help founders turn clarity into chaos. Well, I mean, I've never been in any startup where it's just been all smooth and there's not been any, there's not been any chaos. I mean, it's, anytime you start something, there's typically some chaos until you get things going and you get the system down.
And so they're just looking for founders. And there's a ton of different founders. I'm sure you know, you've got folks that are still trying to get the MVP.
You've got folks who have raised seed funding. You have folks who are on their second or third round of funding. Which stage do you enter? Where do you come in? Where is your skills really needed? If I pitch fractional COO services to, as you said, you're just creating the MVP.
It's like, how likely is that to convert, right? We're still building the idea. We don't need someone to build in a system when we don't even know, you know, this is the final product and this is how we're going to market it. And so I think it's a little bit of a education part on, okay, this is how sales works.
This is how diving into like the marketing aspect of it as well. Once you understand that it becomes a little bit clearer and you're able to start validating, you're able to start testing out and seeing, hey, will people even pay for this? Is this worth me building a business on? And so, yeah, that's why I say it's a little bit of both.

TABARAK: Yeah, it makes sense.
It makes sense. Every morning they're, you know, spending 30, 60 minutes trying to triage their day, prioritize like what's on fire, compensating, working at nights and weekends reactively, not strategically. And that's probably happened to you back in 2022 when you were early on, before you set your boundaries and kind of knew your goal.
Capping clients, I'm hearing a lot between four to five, five to six, you know, I'm usually hearing between three and four retainers and then a lot more advisory roles, right? And the best of the best might have 10 advisory and one, you know, like they're doing a lot of advisory or something like that, or maybe like four or five retainers. And now they're pushing it. We're trying to get more advisory and like, they're just doing a lot of stuff, right? Yeah.
What are your goals get scheduled and then pushed week after week, trying every tool like Notion, Asana, Trello, ClickUp, Spreadsheets, doing everything. And then spending all that time just trying to manually coordinate. And some of it, obviously you got to live in some of the systems of your clients.
It's just how it works, right? You're in their calendar, you're in their system, but you have your own system. And we're seeing a lot of people now using AI or custom automations to glue things together, but it's just hanging on an edge, right? It's like if a new foundation model updates or something happens, like their entire system is going to crash. They're not this, that's just ultimately what we've been seeing, right? And so for us, this is our approach.
We're building something that you can kind of imagine it. It's like Google maps for your work week. It finds the fastest route, reprioritizes around fires.
So you always know what to do exactly next, right? So the core of our thing is that essentially pulls all of your tasks from every system into one place, right? And what it does, it splits them up.
It creates a scope. It understands what the steps are, estimated time for everything.
It scores everything. And essentially what it does, you set up your planning for the week. You set your capacity, you block off your calendar for everything that you don't want to do when you want to go to bed, your date nights or whatever.
So you have your exact amount of time that you want to operate in. And so what happens is everyone has a great plan at the beginning of the week, but things happen. You have a client fire that pops up.
You have to take your kid to the doctor. You got to do X, Y, Z, and then your entire capacity just runs out, right? And so what our system does is on the fly, whenever you bring a new task in, if you have something brand new that comes in from another system, it'll come to the system. We will split it.
We'll grade it. And then we'll ask you, hey, do you want to add it to your capacity? You already made a promise that you're going to work X amount of work. So we reshuffle it.
We rescore everything. And then your lowest capacity will come out for the week. So you still maintain the hours you said you're going to do.
And you do the most important thing next, just like Google Maps, we get you to the next thing, right? And so our hypothesis is that, A, if we can get you doing the next thing correctly, I mean, first, right, that you're going to always be able to manage your day appropriately. And what we also do is we keep track of your goals, your weekly goals, and your client goals as well. And so that's where the scoring comes from.
So if you do have a client fire, you can essentially adjust the toggle and your efforts will go towards that client for a certain amount of time, 24 hours, 48 hours. It'll lower the priority score on everything else, allow you to take care of that, then reshuffle you back into a safe week so that you can get to the end of your week. So that's kind of what we're working on right now.
This is phase one. If we can, our hypothesis is we can get your orchestration and your capacity right. That leads us to then allow us to, what we believe is a job is just a bundle of tasks.
So eventually we can start automating those tasks for you and then offshoot those tasks from your capacity to the AI's capacity. So it learns how you operate and then they'll start doing work for you as we progress.

PHILLIP: Yeah.
I mean, I think the only thing I would say is one big thing is also is figuring out like who they are as a person and their goals. Like why did you get into fractional? Because a lot of folks don't realize how much that actually shapes all the rest of those items that you just talked about. Right.

TABARAK: We actually have a values module. You come in and so you start off with your goals, you start off with your values, you pick family, whatever it is. It actually builds into your score too.
Right. So it's the tiebreaker. You got two things really close and family's important to you.
Boom. It'll shoot it in the right direction. If it's about craft, it's about this.
We really are thinking from the fundamental place of like, I mean, just to give you a little bit of understanding, you might not be our ideal client, but like, I love how you're thinking, but this isn't our main goal. Like we want to start in this wedge. Our goal is to help people become the best versions of themselves.
Right. And the ability to be able to do that first is how do you manage your time? You have to get paid. You have to be able to do your job and your craft first.
That allows you to have the time to now think more about your values, your time, do the thing that you did and allocate your time in the areas that you really want to be in. But I think from a foundational wedge, we're hard workers and we really like to work really hard. Right.
But we also want you to be able to... You're not going to be controlled by the AI overlord, right?
You tell the AI how you want to manage your time and then we get you to that end goal, like Google Maps.

PHILLIP: Exactly. Exactly.
I love that because that's what it is, right? So many folks, they go into fractional, like you said, they're coming from whether it's HR, it's Ops, it's CMO. And a lot of them are coming into the market because of our situations as well.

Does that fit your capacity? And then it says 20% of your time goes towards this goal, 80% of times towards this client, your effective rate is this. STEVEN: And, you know, if I can, A, help influence your product and B, do find the tool potentially that helps me, great. That's awesome, man.

TABARAK: How long have you been a fractional?

STEVEN: About two and a half years.

TABARAK: Okay, well, what led you there? What was your previous life before fractional work?

STEVEN: I was managing an outsourced accounting business. So, we did bookkeeping advisory and tax for startups and small businesses.

STEVEN: Yeah, you know, business breakup. So, we grew that business from the two of us to about 50 people and had different visions of where we wanted to go. And so, it was either kind of like stay and don't be happy or go somewhere else. So, I decided to, you know, break out on my own and stick in this industry. I serve as a fractional CFO, as well as I coach and consultant to accounting firm owners.

TABARAK: That's cool. That's really cool.

STEVEN: Yeah. Yeah. Startup life is not easy. It's difficult every day. And some days it's good. Some days it's down. It's a lot of the down days, too.

TABARAK: Yeah. Yeah. I think that you hit it on the head. The most difficult thing and I think the hardest thing is finding a partner that you can really work with. And I don't know what that answer is. I think there's a lot of vetting that has to happen. And then even when you do that, it's not always going to be a thing, you know. And so, I find it a blessing that you're able to find that early and not drag through it because it could be, you know.

STEVEN: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. We worked out for 10 years.

TABARAK: So, here's a question for you. How do you, how many clients do you manage now in your fraction?

STEVEN: Well, let me give you a little bit more context because as, you know, as me, I have the fractional work and to answer your question directly, there's probably three to four CFO customers and three to four accounting firm customers at a time. But I also, I also teach accounting at Brown University. So, from, you know, my hat as a fractional is not just the business. I've got other things, too. So, I'm not necessarily fractionalizing those other things. But when I, my biggest thing is time and focus. And so, it's not just inside of Open Road, my business, the fractional things that I have to change between customer focus. It's also, I have to focus on, you know, what is my class, what are my classes need at Brown? You know, that's a, you know, that takes time, energy, and focus. You know, I got a small venture capital fund focused on accounting and fintech. So, that takes extra focus and individual time. So, my time is very, like, broken up and I know that I do well when I have focused work time, right? And I have less and less of that every day.

TABARAK: How do you plan your week? You sound like you're really busy.

STEVEN: I rely heavily on my calendar. And another challenge is it's just calendars and accounts, right? So, you know, I've got my Brown email, I got my business email, you know, I work as a subcontractor for a CPA firm. So, I've got an account with them, right? I've got, like, five or six email accounts. I've got to manage calendars and make sure that all those get onto one calendar so that I'm not double booking. So, you know, the short answer is right now the best, like, central place I've got for managing my time is my Google calendar.

TABARAK: Yeah, yeah, yeah. And then I've heard of this story a lot and people that have, do you have other software that you use outside of your calendar to kind of manage?

STEVEN: Yes, I've got G Suite as my primary. I've got some that I'm managing, you know, engagement agreements with, but in terms of, like, client delivery, internally, internal ops, it's mostly Google apps. I am planning to, I just hired an analyst offshore in Mexico, so I'm planning to utilize Slack for that. I mean, I use Slack for other things, but for communications, I use Slack for kind of internal, I'm planning to use Notion for kind of a whole, like, knowledge base.

TABARAK: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Are you a checklist type person? Do you have, like, a sticky note to scratch them off also?

STEVEN: Hell yeah.

TABARAK: I'm starting to see these patterns with people now. Yeah, yeah, yeah. CFO, a lot of things, a lot of different places, checklist with scratch. Okay.

STEVEN: I hate it, but, you know, when it's, you know, it's like a new sheet of paper every day, and, you know, when I try to create digital lists, they just get, they're not in front of me, and they just get lost.

TABARAK: Yeah, yeah. So you have, is there, like, a sweet spot for how many you put on that checklist in a day? Do you have, like, a set number, or do you just put everything that you know you got to do, and then you just scratch off?

STEVEN: More of the latter, but I say, you know, what do I have to get done in the next two days, right?

TABARAK: Yeah, that's fair. Okay, well, how about this? Let me throw some of these questions that I've been having, and here's the last question I'd ask, and you would know better than anyone. STEVEN: Project management.

TABARAK: Yeah, that makes sense. You have a lot of tasks all over the place, and a lot of different systems. How do you prioritize your order of operations? Is it deadlines? Importance?

STEVEN: It's client-based. It's like thinking through the list of clients, you know, what's the next deliverable? What's the next, or what's the next value that I need to create for, you know, each customer? You know, that way, I know I'm not, I'm thinking about each customer every time I'm thinking about what I need to do next.

TABARAK: Yeah, yeah, yeah. So, it sounds like you've got a lot of capacity. Can you see my screen right now?

STEVEN: Yeah.

TABARAK: Okay, cool. So, these are the top challenges that I'm seeing from fractionals right now, and they may all not apply. Some may apply, but the first one is this stuck in this feast or famine cycle. They're working, working, working. Things get on fire. They stop prospecting. They stop going after new customers. They look up, and now there's no clients ready to go when their engagements are done, right? Second one is losing client trust because you have no system for protecting commitments when shit hits the fan during the week, right? So, something goes crazy. One urgent need kind of breaks the whole bundle of everything you're supposed to do, and you're worried about losing trust. Saying no to new opportunities because your setup can't handle more. You feel overwhelmed, anxious, or guilty because you're constantly worried about letting yourself or clients down. Urgent work blows up your week, so you keep busy but not moving forward on the bigger business goals, and then lastly, stitching together a whole bunch of paid tools, but you're still not getting the maximum result. Do you align with any of these?

STEVEN: The bottom of the list, you know, so stuck in feast or famine. Mine tend to be pretty much recurring monthly engagements, so I know there will be churn. I haven't really had significant churn yet. Losing client trust, haven't done that either. You know, shit definitely does hit the fan, but, you know, manage it. Saying no to new opportunities, you know, I probably should be doing more of that. I'm not to the place yet where I am. Feeling overwhelmed because of customer work, definitely. Urgent work blows up the week, probably a third of the time, and then the last one, not really feeling, I mean, yes, feeling that. It's not a huge pain point because I haven't really leaned on the tools yet, and I do have a note taker associated in this meeting, but I do like to.

TABARAK: What would you rank one, two, and three?

STEVEN: One is feeling overwhelmed and anxious. Two, I would say the last one. Even though I said that wasn't as much of a pain point, I think if I were to prioritize things, that would be a higher priority because I think it can help solve the other two things.

TABARAK: Okay, cool. Have you found any of the solutions to these? I mean, you're not stuck in feast or famine, so I'm assuming when I hear that, I hear that the fractional is typically found, they have a good grasp on what type of clients they like to work with and who they are as well, and which means that they are getting the type of engagements they like to work on, right? So there's a hefty amount of work that allows you to stay in the engagements. Either you're doing longer engagements, or are you just finding a way to prospect and get your clients more stable?

STEVEN: Stay in the engagements more.

TABARAK: Yeah, that's cool.

STEVEN: You'll go through a phase of you leverage your network, and your business comes through a network. And then at a certain point, you kind of consume that pent up demand. And then there was a period where I was definitely feeling the need for more pipeline, and not having a whole lot of pipeline. How do I find the customers that need my services that don't already know me? That's where plugin comes in. And then it's kind of the referral called the referral engine turned on. So I got a number of referrals from customers. So that's been feeding enough at this point. But at some point, I will need more pipeline.

TABARAK: Yeah, but that's not current challenge. So when the pipeline, when you sniff it out that you don't need it, you go back into prospect?

STEVEN: I like video. I don't do I don't. My challenge there is staying on cadence. Yeah, right. Like, you know, delivering it twice a week, delivering it once a week, whenever your cadence is, I fall off and don't do it. So when I've been successful with that is when I have a team to help me with that. I don't have a team right now.

TABARAK: That's pretty cool. Do you do more like phone in face? You just do talking head? Or is it like full production?

STEVEN: Talking head?

TABARAK: Okay, that's cool. Because I know that if I try to do full production, I'll never get anything out the door.

STEVEN: Yeah. And I think what people like the authenticity of, you know, phone in face.

It actually helps you write your scripts. So it might be something that helps if you want to get back on cadence with that. Vid IQ?

STEVEN: Yeah. Vid IQ. All right.

TABARAK: Well, here's the, some of the solutions I've been seeing. Telling yourself you do outbound on your calendar and then giving it away when clients need it. Doing 30, 60 minutes of triaging every morning. So it's like, all right, well, let me reorder everything every single day. Figure out what's on fire. Compensating by working nights and weekends reactively, not strategically. Capping clients at three to five clients so you don't overwhelm your process. Quarterly goals getting scheduled and then pushed week after week. Trying every tool, notion, Asana, Trello, kick up, click up. And just manually adjusting for everything. Recently, we've been getting a lot more people using AI and custom automations, but they're not devs. So they build it to a degree, but it could fall apart at any time. Yeah. TABARAK: Yeah. Is there anything specifically here that you do and you're like, oh.

STEVEN: I would say all of the above.

STEVEN: Yeah, yeah, yeah.

TABARAK: Okay, cool. Well, I guess I'll just jump right into kind of what we're working on and kind of give you a heads up so we can talk a little bit more about it. But for us, our approach, just think about Vantom, our product. It's like Google Maps for your work. It finds the fastest route, reprioritizes around fires, so you always know what to do next. So technically, how it works is everything that you just talked about, it understands your goals, your stakeholders. And what we mean by stakeholders is everything that takes against your time, your business, your clients, your schoolwork, family, going to the gym, all of those things we track against your capacity, as well as your actual tasks. And what we do is we break down all of your tasks for the week. We pull all the tasks in from different systems. We score them. And we have an algorithm that rates, you know, all of that based on your inputs for your goals and your stakeholders across your life. And then what it does is you commit to a certain amount of work. So how much work do you, how many hours do you work a week?

TABARAK: Okay. Well, imagine you don't want to work any more than 60 hours a week. Sometimes it's more, sometimes it's less or whatever. If you can commit to 60, I think that's a lot. I'm thinking maybe you'd like maybe 50 if you could, but say 50 hours was the case. Well, you go into planning during the week and your goal is 50 hours. I'm going to do 50 hours. Let me line up everything I have to do. And then what the system does is prioritizes everything for you and you start your week. Well, guess what? That's not like something's going to happen. A client's going to say, I need to do this. School's going to say, I need this for me. This is going to happen. And that has to enter your sprint. So then what the system will do is essentially recalculate everything and move the lowest impactful task out of your sprint for you. You get to choose yes or no, but you commit to the hours that you need and allows you to take care of all the important things that you could do. And obviously if there's a thing that you have to do and you have to keep that in there, you get the choice of doing it, but you get to choose. AI is not the overload that forces everything upon you, right? So ultimately what our system does, it finds the best route for your work week. It prioritizes everything for you and essentially puts things in an order based on your value set, your goals, priorities, your urgency, and it takes all of that risk off of you. # Zach Problem

*Speaker turns were separated using **`anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6`** via OpenRouter. Verify against the recording if a line must be exact.*

TABARAK: Hello, mate. How's it going, Zach? I'm definitely here this time. No worries.

ZACH: I appreciate it, man. Hey, thank you for taking the time and hopping on and rescheduling as well. No, no, no.

TABARAK: All good, man. Sorry, I just completely left you hanging last time. Oh, man.

ZACH: Sounds like you've got a busy schedule. Yeah, yeah. When the little one goes down, all hands on deck.

TABARAK: Yeah, I hear you. So I guess maybe let me just kind of give an outline of what I wanted to go over today. Maybe some quick intros and then essentially give you an overview.

I am a new CEO building a product out right now for Fractions. And so essentially my co-founder and I, we, maybe I'll give you a little bit of background. My name is Tabarik.

I used to do sales in the corporate world for about 20 years. I mean, from Apple to Verizon, and then recently as a regional vice president for new sales at DocuSign for about six or seven years. And so I left that corporate world and naturally in between that world and starting my business now, we started doing some consulting.

And as we started consulting, we found that I was doing a lot more clients for essentially like new startups, helping them with go to market, some sales stuff, some marketing stuff. And we realized it was more than ever just giving them advice. We're just getting in there and actually doing the work.

And I realized I just cannot manage multiple clients and then essentially grow my business, the AI business that I'm trying to work on. Right. And so we found this insight.

We didn't know if it was true. So we started reaching out to a whole bunch of fractionals and kind of getting an idea of like, is this our bias or are people handling the situation in a certain way? Right. So that kind of led me to perusing LinkedIn.

I found yours. And based on some of our hypothesis, it looked like you're really busy. You have a ton of clients that you work with.

You have a foot in building a product plus a community. And so I thought, wow. Well, you know, and then I stumbled.

Yeah. This guy's got the problem, but he could also sell it to another thousand people as well. Let's get this guy on board.

I mean, that's a huge thing, too. I mean, you're a sales guy, right? But the reality was what sealed the deal for me is when I got to your exact your Delphi agent and I started talking to that and I was like, well, I'm getting really good feedback. Let me talk to this guy in person.

Right. Because there's a level of your earned knowledge that you've had and also what you put into the exact box. So I guess the whole purpose of this, I wanted to kind of learn a little bit more about you.

Go over some of the problems I'm seeing in the market and then show you some ways I'm hearing other fractions are dealing with it. Get your kind of advice and take on that and then give you an overview, just a quick overview of what we're trying to build and then see if you have any interest on any next steps. Like, would you want to? Because ultimately, what we really want to do is build a customer development program, get key fractionals in there that are in alignment that would help want to shape the product, get the product in their hands and use it.

Right. So that's kind of my my overview. But what about you, Zach? Why don't you give me a little bit of background about yourself?

ZACH: Yeah, sure.

And look, I think all of your all of your kind of instincts or insights there are bang on. Mate, I'll give you the I'll try to give it as short as possible, but you know how it is with our sales guy, which we like to talk. I've been in tech for a long time.

Tech sales, mostly sales and management. My last real job with a regular salary was for a late stage ad tech company called MediaMath out of Singapore. We came back to Australia just in time for Covid.

We launched AI healthcare startup. Let me put your notes in. Got to get those notes.

Yeah. Yeah. Launched a startup, raised a bit of money, got a couple of clients, never quite managed to hit escape velocity.

And so fast forward into that kind of two years in, I then had two kids under two, was looking to work flexibly remotely from a little seaside town.

TABARAK: Where are you, man? You're in Seattle.

ZACH: No, I moved to Bangkok. I live in Bangkok now.

TABARAK: Oh, you're in Bangkok. I left Seattle, went on this world tour, ended up in Bangkok and realized, well, I can get my life together. Let me let me do what I really want to do. You know, I got an office and set up shop here. I'm just going to chat.

ZACH: Yeah. Mate, that's super interesting. I do know some interesting fractionals for you to talk to in Bangkok, too.

Mostly U.S. expats doing CMO work, living the dream, selling back in with American clients, living like absolute kings, living that arbitrage, that exchange arbitrage dream in Bangkok. So remind me that that's a point to introduce you to those guys as well.

TABARAK: Yeah.

ZACH: So I just started kind of doing fractional work by accident. I'd work with fractional CFOs and even a fractional CTO. I didn't know anyone who was doing fractional sales work.

So I just started shouting about it, picked up a couple of clients, which is obviously good validation. This is about four years ago now. Obviously, it's had a bit of a moment since then and it's kind of popped off.

And along the way, a whole bunch of people reached out. In fact, way more people who wanted to be a fractional rather than hire one. And similar kind of questions, man.

You've poked around on the site, you've done research, you know what it is. It was all the operations side of things. How does this fractional thing work? How do you find clients? How do you split your time? How do you know what to work on? What contract do you use? Things like that.

And so that was the birth of the fractional exec community. Started as a free slack group, grew up a little bit about 18 months ago, evolved to a paid membership model on a dedicated community platform called Mining Networks. Still very much focused on the operational side.

How do people do that? So that's still there. It's still a side hustle. It's 2% of my revenue, if that.

TABARAK: How much of your time?

ZACH: Yeah, way too much. That revenue to time split is fucking wrong, dude. If you look at the stuff that I talk about and all the content, it's way out of whack.

My day job is still fractional go-to-market work. I'll embed a couple of days a week, one or two days a week with a client to scale out their sales and go-to-market engine. So, yeah, mate, that's kind of where I'm at.

In terms of the number one problem that fractionals have, the one they're all dealing with, it's generating pipeline consistently. The feast or famine cycle, the single shingle operator was the catchy phrase that somebody dropped on me, and that rings true. I've got my clients.

I can do plenty of pipe. Then I'm full. I've got two or three clients.
